items.name: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090
items.specs: 21,760 CUDA cores, 32 GB GDDR7, 1,792 GB/s memory bandwidth, 512-bit bus, Blackwell (sm_120), CUDA 12.8+, 575 W TDP, PCIe 5.0 x16, dual-slot (FE) / triple-slot AIB
items.ai_relevance: Top single-card consumer option for local AI. Runs 7B/13B models at full BF16/FP16 with headroom for KV cache; 34B models at Q5/Q8 comfortably; 70B models require Q4_K_M (~38 GB loaded) which barely fits — tight. Dual 5090 over PCIe (no NVLink on consumer) gives 64 GB for 70B at Q4 or Mixtral-class MoE. Does NOT fit 120B solo. Best single-card inference speed for sub-34B workloads in 2026.
items.compatibility: Requires CUDA 12.8+ and PyTorch 2.7.0+ for native sm_120 support (PTX JIT fallback works but is slower on older wheels). Windows/Linux both supported. llama.cpp and Ollama support sm_120 via CUDA backend from early 2025 builds. No NVLink on consumer SKU; multi-GPU is PCIe-only. 16-pin (12VHPWR) power connector required. PSU 1000 W+ recommended.
items.price_band_usd: $2,000 MSRP (FE, rarely in stock at MSRP); street $2,900-$4,300+ depending on AIB/availability; severe supply constraints as of June 2026

items.name: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5080
items.specs: 10,752 CUDA cores, 16 GB GDDR7, 960 GB/s memory bandwidth, 256-bit bus, Blackwell (sm_120), CUDA 12.8+, 360 W TDP, PCIe 5.0 x16
items.ai_relevance: Strong mid-tier for local LLMs. 16 GB VRAM runs 7B/13B at BF16, 34B at Q4_K_M comfortably, and can partially offload 70B to CPU RAM via llama.cpp. Bandwidth advantage over Ada 4080 makes token generation noticeably faster on identical models. Good for Stable Diffusion XL / Flux.1 with headroom. 5080 vs 5070 Ti: same VRAM ceiling but 20% more compute and 7% more bandwidth.
items.compatibility: Same sm_120/PyTorch 2.7.0+ requirement as 5090. PTX JIT libnvptxcompiler.so issue reported on CUDA 12.8/12.9 — use CUDA 12.9.1+ or Docker cu128 image to avoid. 16-pin power; 850 W PSU minimum.
items.price_band_usd: $999 MSRP; street $1,100-$1,800 June 2026 (supply constrained)

items.name: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5070 Ti
items.specs: 8,960 CUDA cores, 16 GB GDDR7, ~896 GB/s memory bandwidth, 256-bit bus, Blackwell (sm_120), CUDA 12.8+, 300 W TDP, PCIe 5.0 x16
items.ai_relevance: Same 16 GB VRAM ceiling as 5080 at lower compute. Runs 7B/13B at BF16 fully in VRAM; 32B-34B at Q4. Tensor Core throughput lower than 5080 (~280 vs 336) — slightly slower token generation on large models. Good value if found near MSRP, but supply has pushed it to near-5080 pricing eliminating the value case. Suitable for Stable Diffusion, fine-tuning <13B with LoRA.
items.compatibility: Identical sm_120 CUDA 12.8+/PyTorch 2.7.0+ requirements. 300 W TDP needs 850 W PSU. 16-pin connector.
items.price_band_usd: $749 MSRP; street $880-$1,200 June 2026 (prices nearly doubled from launch per FPSReview; poor value vs 5080 at current spreads)

items.name: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5070
items.specs: 6,144 CUDA cores, 12 GB GDDR7, 672 GB/s memory bandwidth, 192-bit bus, Blackwell (sm_120), CUDA 12.8+, 250 W TDP, PCIe 5.0 x16
items.ai_relevance: 12 GB VRAM is the main constraint — only 7B and smaller models run at BF16; 13B requires Q6/Q8, 34B requires aggressive Q4 with some CPU offload. Faster per-token than Ada 4070 Ti/4080 due to GDDR7 bandwidth jump. Fine for Stable Diffusion SDXL, fast 7B chat, coding assistants. Not recommended for 34B+ primary workflows.
items.compatibility: sm_120, PyTorch 2.7.0+, CUDA 12.8+. llama.cpp works out of the box with recent builds. 250 W TDP; 750 W PSU sufficient. 16-pin adapter may be required on some AIBs.
items.price_band_usd: $549 MSRP; street ~$600-$700 June 2026

items.name: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5060 Ti (16 GB variant)
items.specs: 4,608 CUDA cores, 16 GB GDDR7, 448 GB/s memory bandwidth, 128-bit bus, Blackwell (sm_120), CUDA 12.8+, 180 W TDP, PCIe 5.0 x8
items.ai_relevance: The unusual case: more VRAM than the 5070 (16 GB vs 12 GB) at lower compute and half the bandwidth. The 16 GB tier lets you fit 13B at BF16 or 34B at Q4 entirely in VRAM — a capability gap vs the 5070. However, narrower 128-bit bus means lower tokens/sec than 5070 for the same model. Best for VRAM-limited workloads (fine-tuning 7B-13B LoRA, embedding batch processing) where bandwidth is less critical than fitting the model. The 8 GB variant is not recommended for AI.
items.compatibility: sm_120 / CUDA 12.8+ / PyTorch 2.7.0+. PCIe 5.0 x8 (not x16) — negligible difference for inference. Single 8-pin power; works in compact systems. Launched May 19, 2025.
items.price_band_usd: 16 GB: $429 MSRP; street $487-$610 June 2026. 8 GB: $379 MSRP; avoid for AI use.

items.name: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5060
items.specs: 3,840 CUDA cores, 8 GB GDDR7, 448 GB/s memory bandwidth, 128-bit bus, Blackwell (sm_120), CUDA 12.8+, 145 W TDP, PCIe 5.0 x8
items.ai_relevance: Entry-level AI option. 8 GB VRAM strictly limits to 7B models at Q4-Q6 (7B Q4_K_M ~4.1 GB fits fine; 7B BF16 at ~14 GB does not). Adequate for Stable Diffusion 1.5 / SDXL at 512-1024px. Not suitable for 13B+ models, coding assistants loading large context, or fine-tuning. The GDDR7 bandwidth is a generational improvement over RTX 3060 12 GB but VRAM cap makes it a poor long-term AI investment.
items.compatibility: sm_120 / CUDA 12.8+ / PyTorch 2.7.0+. Single 8-pin. 145 W TDP; compatible with most 550 W+ PSUs. Released May 19, 2025.
items.price_band_usd: $299 MSRP; street $299-$360 June 2026 (closest to MSRP of any 50-series)

items.name: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell (Workstation Edition)
items.specs: 24,064 CUDA cores, 96 GB GDDR7 ECC, 1,792 GB/s memory bandwidth, Blackwell (sm_120 / GB202), CUDA 12.8+, 600 W TDP, PCIe 5.0 x16, dual-slot DFT cooler, 5th-gen Tensor Cores with FP4 support
items.ai_relevance: Only sub-$15K single GPU that fits 70B models at FP8/BF16 without quantization (70B FP8 uses ~70 GB, leaving ~26 GB for KV cache). Fits Llama 3.1 405B at Q2 or 120B at Q4-Q5. Benchmarked at 163 tok/s on 120B models. Single PRO 6000 matches 4x RTX 4090 throughput on 30B AWQ at lower total power draw and single PCIe slot. ECC memory critical for long-running inference servers. FP4 Tensor Cores yield up to 3x speedup vs prior generation for quantized workloads.
items.compatibility: sm_120 / CUDA 12.8+ / PyTorch 2.7.0+ (same Blackwell wheel requirements as consumer). ECC requires workstation motherboard or server platform (XEON/Threadripper Pro recommended). 600 W TDP requires 1200 W+ server PSU or workstation-class power. Windows and Linux supported. Works with vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, llama.cpp (CUDA backend), Ollama. No NVLink on workstation edition — pairs via PCIe for multi-GPU.
items.price_band_usd: $8,565 MSRP (launch March 2025); street $8,000-$13,250 June 2026 (Newegg lists at $13,250; Amazon ~$9,200)

items.name: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 (Ada Lovelace)
items.specs: 16,384 CUDA cores, 24 GB GDDR6X, 1,008 GB/s memory bandwidth, 384-bit bus, Ada Lovelace (sm_89), CUDA 11.8+, 450 W TDP, PCIe 4.0 x16
items.ai_relevance: Still the proven local AI workhorse in 2026 with mature ecosystem support. 24 GB runs 7B/13B at BF16, 34B at Q4/Q5, and Mixtral 8x7B entirely in VRAM. 70B requires Q4 + CPU offloading (fits in 24 GB at Q3_K_M ~20 GB). Sweet spot is 14B Q8 or 32B Q4_K_M at 40-70+ tok/s. All frameworks (PyTorch, JAX, TF, llama.cpp, vLLM) ship stable sm_89 wheels — no toolchain friction. Discontinued production; supply dwindling and prices rising. Used market is strong option.
items.compatibility: sm_89 (Ada Lovelace) / CUDA 11.8+ / PyTorch 1.13+. Maximum compatibility — every major AI framework has stable wheels. No Blackwell-specific features (FP4 Tensor Cores, 5th-gen Tensor). 16-pin (12VHPWR) power; 850-1000 W PSU. No NVLink on consumer RTX 4090. Triple-slot cooler. Runs hot under sustained inference — good airflow required.
items.price_band_usd: $1,599 original MSRP (EOL); street $2,400-$3,775 new, $1,900-$2,500 used/refurbished June 2026

items.name: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4080 Super (Ada Lovelace)
items.specs: 10,240 CUDA cores, 16 GB GDDR6X, 736 GB/s memory bandwidth, 256-bit bus, Ada Lovelace (sm_89), CUDA 11.8+, 320 W TDP, PCIe 4.0 x16
items.ai_relevance: 16 GB Ada card; capable of 7B/13B at BF16, 32B-34B at Q4_K_M. Slower than RTX 5080 (lower bandwidth, older Tensor Cores) but has zero toolchain friction — stable wheels everywhere, mature driver stack. Good value on secondary market. Same VRAM ceiling as RTX 5080 but ~23% less bandwidth means noticeably slower token generation for large models. Suitable for coding assistants, Stable Diffusion, fine-tuning small LoRA adapters.
items.compatibility: sm_89 / CUDA 11.8+ / PyTorch 1.13+. Excellent compatibility. 320 W TDP; 750 W PSU minimum. 16-pin adapter included in box. Still in limited production/retail.
items.price_band_usd: $999 MSRP; street $1,100-$1,800 new June 2026; ~$795 used

summary: As of June 2026, the NVIDIA local-AI GPU landscape is dominated by the Blackwell RTX 50-series (sm_120, CUDA 12.8+, PyTorch 2.7.0+ required) with GDDR7 delivering 40-75% bandwidth improvements over Ada Lovelace (sm_89). The critical cross-cutting issues are: (1) supply constraints — every 50-series card sells above MSRP, with the RTX 5090 reaching 2x MSRP; (2) the sm_120 toolchain matured in PyTorch 2.7.0 (stable sm_120 wheels) but older projects may need rebuild or nightly builds; (3) VRAM remains the hard ceiling — for 70B+ models without quantization only the RTX PRO 6000 (96 GB, $8K-$13K) fits on one card, while the 32 GB RTX 5090 squeezes 70B at Q4 with almost no headroom. For most developers: the RTX 5090 is the performance leader under $5K; the RTX 5080 or 5060 Ti 16GB are the sanity picks at 16 GB; the RTX 4090 remains a strong secondary-market option with zero friction for users already invested in CUDA toolchains. Dual-GPU (PCIe, no NVLink on consumer) works in llama.cpp/vLLM but adds latency overhead vs single-card.

items.name: NVIDIA DGX Spark (GB10 Founders Edition)
items.specs: GB10 Grace Blackwell Superchip: 20-core Arm CPU (10x Cortex-X925 + 10x Cortex-A725, aarch64), Blackwell GPU (6,144 CUDA cores, sm_121), 128 GB LPDDR5X unified memory, 273 GB/s bandwidth, 1 PFLOP FP4 sparse / 31 TFLOPS FP32, NVLink-C2C CPU-GPU interconnect (~5x PCIe Gen5 bandwidth), 4 TB NVMe Gen5 SSD, 140 W TDP. CUDA 13 required.
items.ai_relevance: Primary platform for desktop LLM inference/fine-tune up to ~200B params (128 GB unified); two-unit NVLink cluster reaches ~400B params. Supports llama.cpp (builds from source, GPU-accelerated), Ollama v0.20.5+ (first-class support), SGLang, and NVIDIA NIM containers. vLLM and other CUDA-12-compiled wheels require source-builds or nightly aarch64 wheels due to sm_121/CUDA-13 ABI gaps.
items.compatibility: OS: NVIDIA DGX OS (Ubuntu-based Linux), aarch64 only — no Windows. CUDA 13 + sm_121: pre-compiled x86-64 PyTorch/vLLM wheels do NOT work; use cu130 aarch64 nightly PyTorch wheels. vLLM stable releases built against CUDA 12 produce libcudart.so.12 import errors; workaround is --enforce-eager (loses ~20-30% speed). Ollama v0.20.5 works out of box. llama.cpp compiles fine from source with CUDA flags. Ecosystem maturing but still has gaps as of mid-2026.
items.price_band_usd: $4,699 (Founders Edition, 4TB; price raised from $3,999 in Feb 2026 due to LPDDR5X supply constraints)

items.name: MSI EdgeXpert (GB10)
items.specs: Same GB10 SoC as DGX Spark: 20-core Arm CPU, Blackwell GPU (sm_121), 128 GB LPDDR5X unified memory, 273 GB/s bandwidth, 1 PFLOP FP4 sparse, 140 W TDP. 1TB/4TB NVMe Gen4 SSD options. WiFi 7, BT 5.3. Ships with NVIDIA DGX OS.
items.ai_relevance: Identical LLM inference capability to DGX Spark (same SoC): runs models up to ~200B params. Lowest entry price in the GB10 partner stack. Same software stack and compatibility constraints as DGX Spark. Good for developers wanting GB10 at minimum spend.
items.compatibility: aarch64 + CUDA 13 + sm_121: identical wheel/toolchain constraints to DGX Spark. NVMe Gen4 (vs DGX Spark Gen5) means model loading is ~25% slower than DGX Spark per benchmarks. DGX OS (Linux only). No Windows support.

items.name: ASUS Ascent GX10 (GB10)
items.specs: GB10 SoC (same as DGX Spark): 20-core Arm CPU, Blackwell GPU (sm_121), 128 GB LPDDR5X unified memory, 273 GB/s bandwidth, 1 PFLOP FP4 sparse, 140 W TDP. Chassis: 150x150x51mm, 1.48 kg. Integrated ConnectX-7 NIC + 10GbE NIC. WiFi 7, BT 5.4. 1TB/2TB/4TB NVMe (Gen4 on 1TB/2TB, Gen5 on 4TB). Supports OpenClaw, NemoClaw, stackable chassis.
items.ai_relevance: Same GB10 LLM capabilities as DGX Spark. ConnectX-7 NIC is notable for multi-node clustering. Stackable chassis design allows NVLink-connected clusters. Benchmarked identically to other GB10 units at ~61 tok/s on Qwen 34B. Reported to trigger thermal throttling events more frequently than competing units under sustained load.
items.compatibility: Same aarch64/CUDA 13/sm_121 constraints as all GB10 units. Ubuntu Linux (DGX OS). No Windows. Thermal throttling is a documented issue on sustained workloads. Gen4 SSD on entry config slightly slower than DGX Spark Gen5.
items.price_band_usd: $3,099 (1TB) to ~$4,700 (4TB, prices fluctuating)

items.name: Dell Pro Max with GB10
items.specs: GB10 SoC: 20-core Arm CPU (10x Cortex-X925 + 10x Cortex-A725), Blackwell GPU (sm_121), 128 GB LPDDR5X unified memory, 273 GB/s bandwidth, 1 PFLOP FP4 / 170 TFLOPS FP16, 140 W TDP. Metal construction. 4 TB NVMe Gen4 SSD. 5.9-inch square footprint, ~2.8 lbs. Ships with NVIDIA DGX OS.
items.ai_relevance: Same GB10 inference capabilities as other units; supports up to ~200B params solo or ~400B params when two units are NVLink-connected. Dell positions it for enterprise/federal AI at the edge. Premium build quality. Identical software stack and compatibility constraints to other GB10 boxes.
items.compatibility: aarch64 + CUDA 13 + sm_121: same wheel gap issues as all GB10 units. Gen4 NVMe (not Gen5 like DGX Spark Founders Edition). DGX OS Linux only. Dell enterprise support channel differentiates it from other GB10 units for commercial buyers.
items.price_band_usd: $3,699 (2TB) to $4,600+ (4TB); pricing varies by config on Dell.com

items.name: NVIDIA Jetson AGX Thor Developer Kit
items.specs: Jetson T5000 module: 14-core Arm Neoverse-V3AE CPU, Blackwell GPU (2,560 CUDA cores, 96 5th-gen Tensor Cores), 128 GB LPDDR5X unified memory, 273 GB/s bandwidth, 2,070 TFLOPS FP4 sparse / 1,035 TFLOPS FP8. 40-130 W TDP. QSFP28 (4x25GbE), 5GbE, USB-A, USB-C, HDMI, DP. 1TB NVMe SSD. JetPack 7 / CUDA 13, driver version 580+.
items.ai_relevance: 7.5x the AI compute of Jetson AGX Orin; designed for humanoid robotics and physical AI. 128 GB unified memory supports mid-size LLMs (7B-70B class) for on-robot inference. JetPack 7 bundles CUDA 13, cuDNN, TensorRT, DeepStream for vision+NLP pipelines. Not a desktop dev-box use case — intended for robotics integration. Same CUDA 13/aarch64 wheel maturity caveats as GB10.

Community llama.cpp and Ollama support present. Neoverse-V3AE CPU is a server-class core (different from GB10's Cortex-X925) — some ARM optimizations differ. Not for desktop use: no standard PCIe expansion, purpose-built carrier board.
items.price_band_usd: $3,499 (developer kit with carrier board)

items.name: NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin Developer Kit (64GB)

items.ai_relevance: Production-proven Ampere edge platform. 64 GB unified memory runs 7B-34B models comfortably with llama.cpp/Ollama. JetPack 6 ecosystem is mature — most aarch64 CUDA-12 wheels work without source-builds. Widely used for edge inference, CV pipelines, ROS2 robots. Can emulate all smaller Orin modules (Orin NX, Orin Nano) for testing lower-power configs.

More software-mature than Jetson Thor. Linux only (Ubuntu 22.04 JetPack). PCIe Gen4 x8 slot available on dev kit carrier board for NVMe/NICs.

items.name: Apple Mac mini (M4 Pro, 48GB)
items.kind: Desktop SoC AI box (Apple Silicon, Metal/MLX)

items.ai_relevance: Best price/performance for local LLMs on Apple Silicon at this tier. Runs Llama 3.1 70B (Q4) comfortably. Inference via Ollama 0.19+ (now MLX-backed on Mac), llama.cpp Metal, LM Studio. Ollama MLX backend (added March 2026) delivers ~2x speedup over prior llama.cpp Metal path on sub-14B models. Neural Engine accelerates smaller models. ~58-87 tok/s on 7B models, ~20-30 tok/s on 70B at Q4.
items.compatibility: macOS only. Metal GPU compute — no CUDA, no ROCm. MLX (Apple's framework) and llama.cpp Metal are the primary inference paths. PyTorch MPS backend works but slower than MLX for inference. Most Hugging Face transformers work via PyTorch MPS. No CUDA wheel compatibility — requires Metal-native or CPU fallback. ARM64 (Apple Silicon) — many Linux/CUDA-only tools require workarounds.
items.price_band_usd: $1,399-$1,999 depending on storage config (48GB M4 Pro base ~$1,399 via Apple; CDW/retail varies)

Excellent for day-to-day LLM inference, fine-tuning small models with MLX, and multimodal workflows. Ollama MLX backend (March 2026) significantly improves throughput.

Same Metal/MLX/llama.cpp stack as Mac mini. 546 GB/s bandwidth is still less than half of M3 Ultra's 819 GB/s, so large model (70B+) inference is noticeably slower than M3 Ultra. Thunderbolt 5 enables eGPU or fast external NVMe but no CUDA compute via eGPU on macOS.
items.price_band_usd: $1,999 (base M4 Max 36GB) to ~$3,299 (M4 Max 128GB when available); current configs top at 96GB

summary:
As of June 2026, the unified-memory AI box market splits into two distinct ecosystems: NVIDIA GB10 (Grace Blackwell) units and Apple Silicon Macs.

GB10 units (DGX Spark, MSI EdgeXpert, ASUS Ascent GX10, Dell Pro Max) all share the identical GB10 SoC — 128 GB LPDDR5X at 273 GB/s, sm_121 Blackwell GPU, 1 PFLOP FP4, CUDA 13, aarch64. Key differentiators are NVMe tier (only DGX Spark ships Gen5, giving ~25% faster model loads), thermals (ASUS GX10 throttles under sustained load), build quality, and enterprise support. The critical software trap is that CUDA-12-compiled x86-64 wheels (PyTorch stable, vLLM stable) do not run on aarch64+CUDA 13; developers must use cu130 aarch64 nightly PyTorch wheels or build vLLM from source. Ollama v0.20.5+ and llama.cpp (source build) are the most friction-free inference paths. DGX Spark Founders Edition price rose to $4,699 in February 2026 due to LPDDR5X supply constraints; partner units range $2,999-$4,700.

Apple Silicon Macs run Metal/MLX — no CUDA at all, but the ecosystem is more mature for plug-and-play LLM inference. Ollama 0.19 (March 2026) adopted MLX as its Mac backend, delivering roughly 2x speedup on sub-14B models. The M3 Ultra Mac Studio leads on bandwidth at 819 GB/s (3x the GB10) and can load 200B+ parameter models in its 256 GB config (512 GB removed March 2026 due to DRAM shortage). The M4 Max Mac Studio at 546 GB/s hits ~87 tok/s on 7B models. The M4 Pro Mac mini (48 GB, 273 GB/s) is the price/performance sweet spot for 70B inference at ~$1,400-1,999. Primary Apple trap: AWQ/GPTQ-only models need conversion to GGUF or MLX format before use.

The Jetson AGX Thor ($3,499) matches GB10's memory config (128 GB, 273 GB/s) but targets robotics/physical AI, not desktop dev use. The Jetson AGX Orin ($1,999, 64 GB, 205 GB/s, sm_87/JetPack 6) remains the most software-mature NVIDIA embedded option with fully stable CUDA-12 aarch64 wheel support.

items.name: AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 (Strix Halo) — mini PC / workstation
items.specs: 16-core Zen 5 CPU + 40 CU RDNA 3.5 iGPU (Radeon 8060S, gfx1151); up to 128 GB LPDDR5X-8000 unified memory; 256 GB/s memory bandwidth; XDNA 2 NPU 50 TOPS; configurable 55-120 W TDP; 4 nm TSMC
items.ai_relevance: Primary use case: running 70B parameter models locally on a single device — the only consumer platform that can hold a 70B BF16 model in unified memory. Achieves ~32 tok/s on Llama 3.3 70B Q4_K_M, ~14 tok/s at BF16. Llama 4 Scout 109B (MoE) at ~15 tok/s. Suitable for 7B–70B inference; 128B MoE models possible. Outclasses all single consumer discrete GPUs (max 24 GB VRAM) on large-model capacity. Poor for image/video generation vs discrete RTX 4090 (3-4x slower). AMD Lemonade server and llama.cpp both supported.
items.compatibility: Linux: ROCm 7.x with gfx1151 target; build llama.cpp with -DGGML_HIP=ON -DAMDGPU_TARGETS=gfx1151 -DGGML_HIP_ROCWMMA_FATTN=ON. gfx1151 is NOT on AMD's official ROCm support matrix but is functional and community-documented; ROCm 7.2 upgrade guides exist. Vulkan backend works on Windows and Linux. Windows ROCm support patchy — Linux strongly preferred for GPU-accelerated inference. XDNA 2 NPU: supported via AMD Lemonade + ONNX Runtime for attention offload; llama.cpp routes to iGPU not NPU by default.
items.price_band_usd: $1,499–$3,000+ for mini PCs (64 GB config ~$1,499; 128 GB config ~$2,000–$3,000). GMKtec EVO-X2 128GB/2TB ~$1,999–$3,000; Beelink GTR9 Pro 128GB ~$2,399; Minisforum MS-S1 Max ~$2,959 at Newegg (was $5,699). Framework Desktop 128GB ~$1,999. Laptop form factor (e.g., ASUS ROG Zephyrus with AI Max+ 395) varies.

items.name: AMD Ryzen AI Max 390 (Strix Halo, non-plus) — laptop / mini PC
items.specs: 12-core Zen 5 CPU + 32 CU RDNA 3.5 iGPU (Radeon 8050S, gfx1151); up to 128 GB LPDDR5X unified memory; ~200 GB/s memory bandwidth; XDNA 2 NPU 50 TOPS; 4 nm TSMC
items.ai_relevance: Same gfx1151 architecture as the Max+ 395 but with 20% fewer GPU CUs and 2 fewer CPU cores. Still capable of 70B unified-memory inference, though with lower throughput (~20% slower GPU-bound tasks). More common in thin-and-light laptops. Practical for 7B–70B models; sweet spot is 7B–34B at full quality without quantization pressure on 128 GB configs.
items.compatibility: Identical ROCm/Vulkan software stack to Max+ 395 (gfx1151 target). Same Linux-preferred caveat applies. ASUS ROG Zephyrus and other laptop OEMs shipping this in 2025-2026.
items.price_band_usd: $1,200–$2,500 in laptop form factor depending on OEM and configuration. Mini PC configs less common than 395.

items.name: AMD Ryzen AI 300 series (Strix Point) — laptop APU
items.specs: Zen 5 CPU (up to 12 cores) + RDNA 3.5 iGPU (12 CU, gfx1150); 16–64 GB LPDDR5X shared memory; XDNA 2 NPU 50 TOPS; ~100-120 GB/s memory bandwidth; sub-28 W TDP typical
items.ai_relevance: Entry-level Strix; the iGPU handles LLM inference via Vulkan or ROCm — NPU mostly idle in llama.cpp workflows by default. Practical for 7B–13B quantized models. AMD Lemonade server adds hybrid NPU+iGPU execution for improved prompt processing. A known issue exists where Ryzen AI 350 (Strix Point) defaults to CPU-bound inference in some configurations instead of GPU-accelerated — requires explicit backend targeting. Not suited for 30B+ models due to memory ceiling.
items.compatibility: llama.cpp Vulkan backend functional on Windows and Linux. ROCm for gfx1150 less mature than gfx1151. AMD Lemonade (open-source, OpenAI-compatible) is the recommended inference server for hybrid NPU+GPU execution on this platform. IPEX-LLM not applicable (AMD, not Intel).
items.price_band_usd: Laptop-only; mid-range laptops $800–$1,500. No dedicated mini PC market at this tier.

items.name: AMD Radeon RX 9070 XT (RDNA 4) — discrete GPU
items.specs: 64 CU RDNA 4 (gfx1201); 16 GB GDDR6 on 256-bit bus; 645 GB/s memory bandwidth; 128 AI accelerators; 2.97 GHz boost; 304 W TBP; PCIe 5.0 x16
items.ai_relevance: Best AMD value discrete GPU for local LLM as of mid-2026. 16 GB VRAM fits 7B unquantized (BF16/FP16) or 13B–34B quantized models. Bandwidth advantage over RTX 5060 Ti 16GB (645 vs 448 GB/s) makes it competitive for memory-bandwidth-limited inference. Solid for 7B daily use; 13B–34B with Q4/Q5 quantization. RDNA 4 AI accelerators improve over RDNA 3 for INT8 workloads. Released early 2026; ecosystem still maturing.
items.compatibility: ROCm 6.4.2+ officially supports gfx1201 (released July 2025). llama.cpp ROCm and Vulkan backends both functional. Vulkan backend works on Windows but carries a 'radv is not conformant' warning (testing-only advisory for RADV driver). ROCm on Linux is the recommended path. Known issue: mmproj GPU idle bug (April 2026, ongoing fix). Windows ROCm support remains limited — Linux strongly preferred. FP8 KV-cache quantization not available on AMD ROCm (CUDA-only feature in 2026).
items.price_band_usd: $649–$800 street (MSRP $699; Gigabyte Gaming OC seen at $649 Amazon, Sapphire Pulse ~$769 Newegg as of June 2026)

items.name: AMD Radeon RX 7900 XTX (RDNA 3) — discrete GPU
items.specs: 96 CU RDNA 3 (gfx1100); 24 GB GDDR6 on 384-bit bus; ~960 GB/s memory bandwidth; 355 W TBP; PCIe 4.0 x16
items.ai_relevance: 24 GB VRAM enables running 34B models quantized or 13B at BF16. ~96 tok/s on Llama 3.1 8B (~75% of RTX 4090). Mature ROCm support (gfx1100 officially supported). The 2026 consensus is: gfx1100 is AMD's best-supported ROCm target for consumer inference — most tutorials, Docker images, and vLLM configs target it. 40–50% cheaper than RTX 4090 for same VRAM tier. Not competitive with RX 9070 XT on bandwidth-per-dollar but has more VRAM.
items.compatibility: gfx1100 is on AMD's official ROCm support matrix — the most reliable AMD consumer GPU for ROCm in 2026. llama.cpp ROCm (LLAMA_HIPBLAS=1) works well on Linux. Windows ROCm remains inconsistent. vLLM now has official AMD support. FP8 KV cache not available. Recommend Ubuntu 22.04/24.04 + ROCm 6.x for best compatibility.
items.price_band_usd: $650–$900 used/open-box (new stock thinning as RX 9070 XT takes over); was $999 at launch

items.name: Intel Arc B580 (Battlemage Xe2) — discrete GPU
items.specs: 20 Xe2 cores + XMX tensor engines; 12 GB GDDR6 on 192-bit bus; 456 GB/s memory bandwidth; 190 W TDP; TSMC 5 nm; PCIe 4.0 x8
items.ai_relevance: Best value Intel discrete GPU for local LLM. 12 GB VRAM fits 7B unquantized or 13B–14B Q4. Achieves ~40–42 tok/s on Llama 3.1 8B via llama.cpp Vulkan; ~32–38 tok/s on Qwen 2.5 14B Q4_K_M via IPEX-LLM on Linux. 27% more bandwidth than RTX 3060 12GB at a lower price. XMX engines accelerate INT8/FP16 matrix math. Best suited for 7B–14B daily inference on Linux.
items.compatibility: Three paths: (1) llama.cpp Vulkan — works Windows/Linux, simplest setup; (2) IPEX-LLM native Linux — best performance, requires conda + Intel GPU drivers, 30-45 min setup; (3) IPEX-LLM Portable ZIP Windows — easiest but slower. Critical: Resizable BAR (ReBAR) must be enabled in BIOS — without it expect 20-25% throughput penalty and potential bus errors. Standard Ollama will not detect Arc GPU; requires IPEX-LLM Ollama fork. Windows delivers ~15–20 tok/s on 14B vs ~32–38 tok/s on Linux (same hardware). Image generation via ComfyUI functional but inconsistent. IPEX-LLM repo archived Jan 2026; community fork continues with nightly builds.
items.price_band_usd: $249 MSRP; ~$299 street (Amazon May 2026); occasionally restocks near MSRP on Newegg

items.name: Intel Arc A770 16 GB (Alchemist Xe-HPG) — discrete GPU
items.specs: 32 Xe-HPG cores + XMX engines; 16 GB GDDR6 on 256-bit bus; ~560 GB/s memory bandwidth; ~225 W TDP; PCIe 4.0 x16
items.ai_relevance: 16 GB VRAM is the key advantage over B580 — fits 13B models comfortably at INT4 and handles some 34B quantized. ~70 tok/s on Mistral 7B with IPEX-LLM INT4; 35+ tok/s on Llama 3.1 8B; 12–18 tok/s on 13B INT4. Better capacity ceiling than B580 at lower bandwidth. Still available at ~$279 making it competitive with B580 for large-model users. Older Alchemist architecture means less driver optimization than B580's Battlemage.
items.compatibility: Same IPEX-LLM / Ollama-fork requirement as B580. ReBAR BIOS requirement applies. Vulkan path works but Alchemist Vulkan driver less polished than Battlemage. IPEX-LLM v2.2.0 (stable) and v2.3.0b (nightly, Ollama 0.9.3) both support A770. Windows vs Linux throughput gap similar to B580. No CUDA; no ROCm (Intel GPU, uses OneAPI/SYCL).
items.price_band_usd: ~$279 (see localaimaster.com; street price relatively stable given continued availability)

items.name: Intel Core Ultra Series 2 (Lunar Lake) — laptop APU
items.specs: Core Ultra 200V series; up to 4P+4E cores; Arc iGPU (Xe2, up to 8 Xe-cores); up to 32 GB LPDDR5X on-package; NPU 4 rated 48 TOPS; platform total ~120 TOPS; ~17–30 W TDP
items.ai_relevance: Copilot+ PC platform. NPU 4 (48 TOPS) is the most capable NPU in any x86 laptop as of late 2025. Intel provides Day-0 NPU support for Qwen3 and other models. IPEX-LLM routes small models (1B–7B) to NPU for battery-efficient inference; larger models spill to iGPU. Practical for sub-7B NPU inference or 7B–13B via iGPU with 32 GB RAM. Not a replacement for discrete GPU; memory ceiling and iGPU bandwidth limit throughput.
items.compatibility: IPEX-LLM supports NPU inference on Core Ultra 200V series. NPU accessed via OpenVINO backend or direct IPEX-LLM NPU path. Ollama via IPEX-LLM fork for iGPU acceleration. The NPU does NOT appear as a standard GPU to llama.cpp — requires Intel-specific tooling. Windows driver support generally solid for iGPU inference; Linux NPU driver less mature.
items.price_band_usd: Laptop-only; $900–$1,600 for Lunar Lake ultrabooks (Dell XPS, Asus Zenbook, etc.)

items.name: Intel Core Ultra Series 3 (Panther Lake) — laptop APU
items.specs: Launched CES January 2026; Intel 18A process; flagship Core Ultra X9 388H: up to 16 CPU cores + 12 Xe3 GPU cores (Arc B390); NPU 5 rated ~50 TOPS; platform total up to 180 TOPS; up to 128 GB DDR5-7200 (NUC Ultra 300 BOX); ~55 W cTDP laptop
items.ai_relevance: Intel's newest APU generation (18A process node). NPU 5 improves TOPS/area by 40%+ vs NPU 4. Arc B390 iGPU (Xe3/Battlemage) delivers higher compute density than Lunar Lake. Intel claims 1.9x local LLM performance improvement. Practical for 7B–13B inference on iGPU with sufficient RAM; NPU handles sub-7B efficiently. ASRock NUC Ultra 300 BOX supports up to 128 GB DDR5, making it viable for larger quantized models via CPU/iGPU combined.
items.compatibility: IPEX-LLM support for Panther Lake NPU and Arc Xe3 iGPU ongoing as of mid-2026 — check nightly builds. Xe3 (Battlemage) iGPU driver more mature than Alchemist. ReBAR still required for best iGPU performance. Windows support better than Linux for NPU at launch; Linux NPU driver catching up. Vulkan path works for iGPU inference in llama.cpp.
items.price_band_usd: Laptops $1,000–$1,800 (availability ramping in 2026); ASRock NUC Ultra 300 BOX mini PC pricing not yet confirmed at research time

items.name: Intel Core Ultra 200H/HX (Arrow Lake-H) — laptop APU
items.specs: Up to Core Ultra 9 285H; Arc iGPU (Xe-LP/Alchemist tier); NPU 3 rated ~13 TOPS; platform total ~99 TOPS; up to 96 GB DDR5; up to 55 W TDP
items.ai_relevance: Mid-generation platform; NPU at 13 TOPS is significantly weaker than Lunar Lake or Panther Lake NPUs — insufficient for practical standalone NPU inference of 7B models. iGPU handles small model inference (1B–7B) reasonably. Mainly relevant as a laptop platform where a discrete Arc or NVIDIA GPU handles LLM work. IPEX-LLM supports NPU on 200H series but workloads are constrained to sub-3B models at practical speeds on the NPU alone.
items.compatibility: IPEX-LLM supports Core Ultra 200H NPU (Python and C++ APIs). iGPU inference via Vulkan or IPEX-LLM. Standard Ollama will not use the Arc iGPU — requires IPEX-LLM fork. Best paired with a discrete Arc GPU (eGPU or in a larger laptop) for meaningful LLM acceleration.
items.price_band_usd: Laptop-only; $900–$2,000+ depending on OEM/config. Mini PCs with 200H available at ~$600–$900 (bare) from NUC-class vendors.

items.name: AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 cluster (multi-node) — workstation array
items.specs: N x Strix Halo nodes connected via llama.cpp RPC; each node: 128 GB unified memory, 256 GB/s bandwidth; aggregate memory scales linearly (2-node = 256 GB, 4-node = 512 GB+)
items.ai_relevance: AMD published a guide (April 2026) for running a 1-trillion parameter LLM locally on a Ryzen AI Max+ cluster using llama.cpp's RPC distributed inference mode (-DGGML_RPC=ON). Aggregate VRAM/memory scales with node count, enabling models (Llama 3 405B, Mixtral 8x22B, future 1T models) that no single consumer device can hold. llama.cpp RPC is the mechanism: each node runs a server, coordinator splits layers across nodes. Practical throughput is bandwidth-limited by inter-node connectivity (10GbE recommended; most mini PCs ship with 10GbE ports).
items.compatibility: Requires llama.cpp built with -DGGML_RPC=ON and -DGGML_HIP=ON on each node. gfx1151 target on all nodes. Linux preferred; Windows ROCm per-node is possible but less tested. Inter-node: 10GbE switch; USB4/Thunderbolt 4 daisy-chain also feasible for 2-node. No special driver needed beyond ROCm on each node.
items.price_band_usd: 2-node cluster from ~$4,000–$6,000 (2x Beelink/GMKtec 128GB configs); 4-node ~$8,000–$12,000

summary: In mid-2026 the AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 (Strix Halo) mini PC is the standout APU platform for local AI: 128 GB unified memory at 256 GB/s bandwidth enables 70B model inference without quantization on a single $2,000–$3,000 device, something no consumer discrete GPU can match. ROCm support for gfx1151 is functional but not on AMD's official support matrix — Linux with ROCm 7.x is the reliable path; Windows ROCm remains patchy. The non-plus AI Max 390 covers the same ground in thinner laptops with 20% less GPU throughput. For discrete AMD GPUs: the RX 9070 XT (RDNA 4, 16 GB, 645 GB/s, ~$650–$800) is the best value inference card with ROCm 6.4.2+ support for gfx1201, though the RDNA 4 software ecosystem is still maturing; the RX 7900 XTX (24 GB, gfx1100) remains the most ROCm-mature AMD consumer option. On the Intel side: the Arc B580 ($249–$299, 12 GB, 456 GB/s) is a capable budget discrete GPU but requires IPEX-LLM (not stock Ollama), mandatory ReBAR BIOS config, and delivers half the throughput on Windows vs Linux. The Arc A770 16 GB (~$279) offers more VRAM headroom for 13B models. Panther Lake (Core Ultra Series 3, launched CES 2026 on Intel 18A) advances the NPU to 50 TOPS and iGPU to Xe3/Battlemage, making it the most capable Intel APU for local AI, but software ecosystem is still catching up. Lunar Lake (Core Ultra 200V) NPU 4 at 48 TOPS has the most mature Intel NPU software stack. Across all non-NVIDIA platforms the consistent theme: Linux is strongly preferred, software setup is more complex than CUDA, and Windows support lags 3–12 months behind.

items.name: Beelink EQ14 (Intel N150)
items.specs: Intel Twin Lake N150 quad-core up to 3.6 GHz, 16 GB DDR4, 500 GB NVMe (up to 4 TB via 2x M.2), dual 2.5 GbE, Wi-Fi 6, BT 5.2, dual 4K@60Hz, ~6-8W idle, 25W TDP max
items.price_band_usd: $190-$220 (configured); $82 barebone
items.ai_relevance: Entry-level control-plane node or Docker/Proxmox service host. Not suitable for LLM inference beyond tiny models (Ollama phi-2 or similar). Intel UHD iGPU (24 EU) has no practical GPGPU story. Best use: Pi-hole, Home Assistant, Nextcloud, k3s worker, network services. ~$15/year electricity.
items.compatibility: Linux/Windows 11 — standard x86-64, no wheel traps. iGPU is Intel Xe/Gen12 — OpenCL works but no CUDA/ROCm. RAM is soldered DDR4 (not upgradable on most SKUs). Check specific listing for upgradable SODIMM variants.

items.name: Beelink EQi3-N305 / EQ12 Pro (Intel N305)
items.specs: Intel N305 8-core up to 3.8 GHz (Alder Lake-N), 16 GB DDR5, dual Ethernet (2.5 GbE), NVMe SSD, ~12-18W idle, 15W TDP
items.price_band_usd: $220-$350 depending on RAM/SSD configuration
items.ai_relevance: Preferred budget Proxmox cluster node or k3s control plane. 8 cores handle multiple VMs simultaneously. Intel iGPU (32 EU) supports Quick Sync for Jellyfin transcoding. Not suitable for LLM inference; no meaningful NPU. Good for: Proxmox with 3-5 VMs, heavy container stacks, homelab NAS.
items.compatibility: Full Linux compatibility, Proxmox certified community builds common. No ROCm/CUDA. Quick Sync works under Jellyfin/Plex via VA-API on Linux. Standard x86-64; all Python/Docker wheels work.

items.name: Beelink SER9 Pro+ (AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370)
items.specs: AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 — 12 cores (4P+8E) Zen 5, up to 5.1 GHz, Radeon 890M iGPU (16 RDNA 3.5 CUs), XDNA 2 NPU 50 TOPS (80 combined TOPS), 32 GB LPDDR5X-8000 (soldered), 1 TB PCIe 4.0 NVMe, dual M.2, USB4, 2.5 GbE, ~28W TDP (54W boost)
items.price_band_usd: $499-$719 (varies by RAM/SSD config and retailer)
items.ai_relevance: Mid-range AI inference node. Runs 7B models at comfortable speeds via llama.cpp (iGPU path, RDNA 3.5 ROCm-lite), 13B Q4 workable, 27B Q4 slow but possible. NPU handles Copilot+ / Windows AI features; llama.cpp NPU offload experimental as of 2026. Good for: Ollama server, local RAG, edge inference for 7-13B models. RAM soldered at 32 GB is the ceiling — limits 70B models.
items.compatibility: Windows 11 / Linux (Debian/Ubuntu). ROCm 6.x supports RDNA 3.5 (gfx1150 target) but wheel availability is still trailing — verify pytorch-rocm wheel for your distro. llama.cpp Vulkan backend works cross-platform. RAM non-upgradable.

items.name: Minisforum AI X1 Pro-470
items.specs: AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 470 — 12 cores Zen 5 up to 5.2 GHz, Radeon 890M (16 RDNA 3.5 CUs), XDNA 2 NPU 55 TOPS (86 combined), upgradable DDR5-5600 SO-DIMM up to 128 GB, 3x M.2 slots (up to 12 TB), OCuLink eGPU port, dual 2.5 GbE, Wi-Fi 7, 195x195x42.5 mm, ~135W PSU integrated
items.price_band_usd: $1,359 (32 GB/1 TB config)
items.ai_relevance: Best-in-class expandable AI workstation mini PC under $1,500. RAM is user-upgradable to 128 GB enabling 70B Q4 LLM inference. OCuLink eGPU slot allows attaching a discrete GPU (e.g., RX 7900 XT) for full ROCm acceleration, making it a viable local AI inference server. Three M.2 slots support large vector DB or model storage. Suited for: production Ollama/LM Studio server, RAG pipelines, small AI cluster head node.
items.compatibility: Windows 11 / Linux. ROCm 6.x for RDNA 3.5 (gfx1150). eGPU via OCuLink — check PCIe bifurcation compatibility for chosen discrete GPU. DDR5 SO-DIMM standard — upgradable. Wi-Fi 7 may need updated firmware on some Linux distros.

items.name: GMKtec EVO-X2 (AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395, Strix Halo)
items.specs: AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 — 16 Zen 5 cores up to 5.1 GHz, Radeon 8060S iGPU (40 RDNA 3.5 CUs), XDNA 2 NPU 40 TOPS, 64 GB or 128 GB LPDDR5X-8000 (soldered, up to 96 GB allocatable to GPU), PCIe 4.0 NVMe, Wi-Fi 7, 230W HuntKey PSU adapter; 8-14W idle, 147-180W under AI/gaming load
items.price_band_usd: $1,399-$1,999 (64 GB config ~$1,399-$1,499; 128 GB ~$1,999; prices have risen since May launch)
items.ai_relevance: Top-tier consumer mini PC for local LLM inference. With 96 GB VRAM allocation, runs Llama 3.3 70B Q6_K and DeepSeek 32B at meaningful token rates fully GPU-accelerated. ServeTheHome confirmed nearly 2x speedup vs CPU-offload mode. Tom's Hardware: comparable to discrete RTX 4060/RX 7600 in graphics tasks. Suited for: production local LLM server, multi-user Ollama endpoint, RAG with large models, homelab AI inference head node. Beats NVIDIA DGX Spark (~$4k) on price-performance per igorslab.de analysis.
items.compatibility: Windows 11 (pre-installed) / Linux. ROCm 6.x — gfx1100 family; PyTorch-ROCm wheels available for Ubuntu 22.04/24.04. llama.cpp ROCm or Vulkan both work. RAM is soldered LPDDR5X — no upgrade path; buy 128 GB config if budget allows. No 10GbE (noted as limitation by ServeTheHome). PCIe 4.0 NVMe only, no OCuLink.

items.name: Minisforum MS-S1 Max (AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395, Strix Halo)
items.specs: AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 — 16 Zen 5 cores, Radeon 8060S (40 RDNA 3.5 CUs), 126 TOPS NPU, 128 GB LPDDR5X-8000 (soldered), 2 TB NVMe, dual 10 GbE LAN, Wi-Fi 7 802.11be, BT 5.4, 160W peak / 130W sustained, 2U rack-mountable chassis option
items.price_band_usd: $2,299 launch price (128 GB/2 TB); has appeared discounted to ~$1,049-$1,199 at Newegg (verify current pricing — volatile)
items.ai_relevance: Professional-grade AI mini workstation. Dual 10 GbE sets it apart from GMKtec EVO-X2 for homelab networking — proper multi-user LLM serving, NAS integration, or cluster interconnect. 2U rack support enables datacenter-adjacent deployment. 128 GB LPDDR5X supports 70B models fully in VRAM. Minisforum markets it explicitly for LM Studio and AI cluster use. Suited for: head node of a homelab AI cluster, multi-user inference endpoint, AI + NAS combined node.
items.compatibility: Windows 11 / Linux. ROCm 6.x (gfx1100). PyTorch-ROCm Ubuntu wheels. RAM soldered — no upgrade. 10 GbE LAN simplifies high-throughput homelab networking vs. competitors. Verified by AkitaOnRails review at 96 GB VRAM config.

items.name: Raspberry Pi 5 (8 GB / 16 GB)
items.specs: Broadcom BCM2712 quad-core Cortex-A76 @ 2.4 GHz, 8 GB or 16 GB LPDDR4X (on-board), microSD + PCIe 2.0 (M.2 HAT needed), 2x USB 3.0, 2x USB 2.0, 1 GbE, dual 4Kp60 HDMI, Wi-Fi 5, BT 5.0, 27W max via USB-C PD, ~3-5W idle
items.price_band_usd: $80 (8 GB) / ~$120 (16 GB at MSRP; real-world prices have risen sharply — $200-$305 seen in Apr 2026 due to LPDDR4 cost inflation). AI HAT+ 2 add-on: $130 (40 TOPS, 8 GB dedicated).
items.ai_relevance: Canonical ARM SBC for edge nodes and distributed homelab fabric. Not fast for LLM inference natively (3-5 tokens/sec for 3B Q8 on 16 GB). With AI HAT+ 2 (40 TOPS, Jan 2026): enables on-device vision AI, small NLP, Frigate NVR object detection at real-time. Best roles: k3s/Kubernetes worker, DNS/network services, Home Assistant, IoT gateway, CI runner, lightweight edge inference with HAT. 20x Pi 5s cost ~$1,600 vs. $5,000+ for Jetson equivalents — cost-efficient cluster fabric.
items.compatibility: Raspberry Pi OS (Debian bookworm aarch64), Ubuntu 24.04 aarch64. Standard aarch64 Python wheels work. AI HAT+ 2 uses Hailo-8L chip — Hailo SDK (HailoRT) required, not llama.cpp compatible natively; separate inference pipeline. No ROCm/CUDA. PCIe 2.0 x1 via HAT limits NVMe bandwidth. Note: price volatility is significant in 2026.

items.name: Radxa ROCK 5B+ (Rockchip RK3588)
items.specs: Rockchip RK3588 — 4x Cortex-A76 + 4x Cortex-A55 octa-core up to 2.4 GHz, Mali-G610 MP4 GPU, 6 TOPS built-in NPU, LPDDR5 RAM options (8/16/24/32 GB), onboard eMMC + M.2 M-key NVMe, 2.5 GbE + 1 GbE, Wi-Fi 6 + BT 5.2, HDMI 2.1, PCIe 3.0, USB 3.2, PoE via HAT, ~3-5W idle
items.price_band_usd: $89 (4 GB, if in stock) / $120 (8 GB) / $159 (16 GB) / $199 (24 GB) — stock availability is inconsistent; Amazon pricing $189-$229
items.ai_relevance: Best-spec Pi-class SBC for homelab edge nodes needing more than Pi 5. RK3588 NPU (6 TOPS) handles Frigate NVR, YOLO object detection, simple speech models via RKNN Toolkit 2 SDK. Mali-G610 GPU has limited OpenCL. PCIe 3.0 NVMe is notably faster than Pi 5's PCIe 2.0. 2.5 GbE standard. Good for: NAS node, k3s worker, Frigate camera AI, Docker host, PoE-powered edge node. Better software documentation than Orange Pi alternatives.
items.compatibility: Radxa official Debian/Ubuntu images (aarch64). RKNN Toolkit 2 for NPU inference — not compatible with generic llama.cpp NPU path; requires RKNN model conversion. Mali-G610 GPU: limited ROCm support (none officially); Panfrost/Lima open-source drivers for display only. Python aarch64 wheels generally work. Stock availability is the main practical problem in 2026.

items.name: NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano Super (Developer Kit)
items.specs: NVIDIA Ampere GPU — 1024 CUDA cores, 32 Tensor Cores; 6-core Arm Cortex-A78AE CPU; 8 GB LPDDR5 unified memory, 102 GB/s bandwidth; 67 INT8 TOPS; 5-25W configurable power modes; 69.9x45mm module on carrier board (260-pin SO-DIMM); microSD + NVMe M.2, USB 3.2, GbE, HDMI, DP, CSI/DSI cameras
items.price_band_usd: $249 (developer kit); has appeared as low as $219 on Slickdeals
items.ai_relevance: The definitive edge AI inference board for CUDA-native workloads. Full JetPack SDK: CUDA 12.x, cuDNN, TensorRT 10, Triton Inference Server. Runs LLM chatbots (3B-7B via TensorRT-LLM), visual AI agents, YOLOv8/v9 real-time, gesture recognition, RAG on-device. 1.7x faster than original Orin Nano. TensorRT INT8 quantization dramatically accelerates inference vs. FP16. Best use: production edge AI deployment, robotics, Frigate + LLM combined, vision pipeline dev, CUDA prototyping without a full GPU server.
items.compatibility: NVIDIA JetPack 6.x (Ubuntu 22.04 aarch64 base). CUDA sm_87 (Ampere). TensorRT wheel ecosystem mature. PyTorch for Jetson available via NVIDIA pip index — do NOT use standard PyPI torch (wrong arch). Docker container runtime works; NVIDIA NGC containers for Jetson. No ROCm/Metal. RAM is unified/shared, not expandable. Power supply: 9-20V DC barrel jack.

items.name: Minisforum MS-01 (Intel Core Ultra / 12th-13th Gen)
items.specs: Intel Core i9-12900H or i9-13900H (16 cores), up to 64 GB DDR5 SO-DIMM (upgradable), 3x M.2 NVMe + 2.5" SATA bay, dual 10 GbE SFP+, dual 2.5 GbE, Thunderbolt 4/USB4, PCIe 4.0, ~20-35W idle
items.price_band_usd: $600-$850 depending on CPU variant and RAM config
items.ai_relevance: Networking-class homelab workhorse. Dual 10 GbE SFP+ is the key differentiator — handles multi-tenant Proxmox cluster networking, TrueNAS/ZFS NAS with high-throughput clients, or LLM serving over fast LAN. No dedicated NPU; iGPU (Intel Xe) not practical for inference. Best role: Proxmox cluster node, pfSense/OPNsense router+NAS, k3s control plane with fast storage I/O, home datacenter backbone. Not an AI inference box — buy it for network/storage, not ML.
items.compatibility: Linux excellent (Proxmox, TrueNAS SCALE, Ubuntu Server all verified). SFP+ requires compatible modules (DAC cables or SFP+ transceivers). Intel iGPU: VA-API Quick Sync for Jellyfin. Thunderbolt 4 eGPU possible for discrete GPU addition. Standard x86-64 — all Python/Docker wheel ecosystems work without issues.

summary: The 2026 mini-PC/SBC homelab market has stratified clearly into three tiers by role. Budget control-plane/service nodes (Beelink EQ14 N150, EQi3-N305, $190-$350) handle Proxmox, Docker, and network services at 6-18W idle with negligible AI capability. Mid-range AI inference nodes (Beelink SER9 Pro+ HX370, Minisforum AIBERZY XG1, $500-$1,400) use AMD Ryzen AI/Strix Point APUs with RDNA 3.5 iGPU + XDNA 2 NPU for comfortable 7-13B LLM serving; the Minisforum AI X1 Pro-470 stands out for having upgradable RAM (up to 128 GB) and an OCuLink eGPU port. The Strix Halo tier (GMKtec EVO-X2, Minisforum MS-S1 Max, $1,399-$2,300+) with Ryzen AI Max+ 395 delivers a genuine workstation-class inference platform — 40 RDNA 3.5 CUs with up to 96 GB allocatable VRAM, capable of Llama 3.3 70B Q6_K inference; the MS-S1 Max adds dual 10 GbE for cluster use. For ARM SBCs: the Raspberry Pi 5 (16 GB + AI HAT+ 2 at 40 TOPS) remains the standard edge node but faced significant 2026 price inflation; the Radxa ROCK 5B+ (RK3588, 6 TOPS NPU, 2.5 GbE, PCIe 3.0) is a stronger spec at $120-$199 with stock issues; the Jetson Orin Nano Super ($249) is the definitive CUDA edge board with full TensorRT/cuDNN/JetPack support at 67 TOPS. ROCm wheel maturity for RDNA 3.5 (gfx1150) is still catching up as of mid-2026 — Vulkan backends are a reliable fallback for llama.cpp workloads on AMD mini-PCs.

items.name: DDR5 32GB (2x16GB) Desktop Kit — Price Trajectory Mid-2025 to June 2026
items.specs: DDR5-6000 CL30 or CL36, 2x16GB SO-DIMM or UDIMM; mainstream Intel/AMD platform target
items.ai_relevance: Minimum capacity for running 7B–13B local LLMs comfortably; pairs with Ryzen 9000 / Intel Arrow Lake builds; 32GB is the new floor for AI-capable desktops
items.compatibility: Intel 700/800-series (LGA1700/1851) and AMD AM5 platforms only. DDR5 is incompatible with DDR4 motherboards. Speed tiers above DDR5-6400 may require XMP/EXPO tuning and are more likely to be affected by shortage-driven substitutions. No CUDA/ROCm dependency but VRAM-limited inference pushes overflow into system RAM.
items.price_band_usd: $350–$520 (June 2026). Was $80–$133 in mid-2025 (Sep 2025: ~$103–$133 per kit). Jan 2026: $513–$616. Floor as of early June 2026 per Techtimes/dropreference: ~$375. Represents a 3.5–4x increase in ~9 months.

items.name: DDR5 64GB (2x32GB) Desktop Kit — Current Market
items.specs: DDR5-6000 or DDR5-6400 CL30/32, 2x32GB UDIMM; supports ECC on select AMD EPYC-lite and some Ryzen Pro platforms
items.ai_relevance: Practical minimum for running 30B–70B quantized models (GGUF Q4/Q5) with 8-bit offload; enables multi-model simultaneous inference; essential for any serious homelab AI node
items.compatibility: AM5 and LGA1851 only. Kingston Fury Renegade 64GB DDR5-6400 available; Crucial 64GB DDR5-4800 also listed but at wildly divergent price points suggesting spot-market volatility. No compatibility issues with standard AI frameworks — PyTorch, llama.cpp, Ollama all see flat system RAM without driver constraints.
items.price_band_usd: $390–$970 (June 2026) — extreme spread reflects market volatility. Kingston Fury Renegade 64GB DDR5-6400: $386–$470 range at major US retailers per June 6 data. Crucial 64GB DDR5-4800: $968 (likely spot/OEM pricing). MicroCenter Trident Z5 Neo 64GB: listed at $895 reduced from $1,399.

items.name: DDR5 128GB (4x32GB or 2x64GB) Desktop / Workstation Kit
items.specs: DDR5-6000 or DDR5-6400, 128GB total; requires 4-DIMM motherboard (X670E, Z790, Z890 or high-end WS); G.Skill Trident Z5 / Corsair Dominator Titanium representative SKUs
items.ai_relevance: Enables running 70B models fully in RAM with CPU inference; required for multi-instance local LLM servers, RAG pipelines with large in-memory indexes, and mixed GPU+CPU offload for 100B+ models
items.compatibility: Only high-end desktop (HEDT) class motherboards with 4+ DIMM slots. Stability at this density requires tight XMP tuning. Newegg bundled G.Skill 128GB DDR5 at $1,459–$1,469 as of June 2026, comparable to RTX 5080 pricing. Z790/Z890 Intel and X670E AMD are the primary targets.
items.price_band_usd: $1,350–$1,500 (June 2026). Newegg listing: $1,459 for G.Skill Trident Z5 128GB kit. Was approximately $400–$600 in mid-2025 at peak-low pricing.

items.name: GDDR7 Supply Shortage and GPU VRAM Cost Impact
items.specs: GDDR7 runs at 28–32 Gbps/pin vs. GDDR6X at 21 Gbps/pin; used in RTX 50-series (5090: 32GB GDDR7, 5080: 16GB GDDR7) and RX 9070-series; VRAM now ~80%+ of total GPU BOM cost
items.ai_relevance: GDDR7 bandwidth is the primary constraint for local LLM inference throughput (tokens/sec scales with memory bandwidth). RTX 5090 at 1,792 GB/s bandwidth is top-tier for sub-70B models. GDDR7 shortage directly inflates GPU prices and limits availability for AI builders.
items.compatibility: RTX 50-series requires PCIe 5.0 x16 for full bandwidth; PCIe 4.0 is functional with minor overhead. CUDA 12.8+ / sm_120 (Blackwell) required for full feature support. ROCm 6.x supports RX 9070 XT (RDNA 4, gfx1201). Driver wheel traps: PyTorch nightly builds required for sm_120 through mid-2026.
items.price_band_usd: RTX 5090 MSRP $1,999 but street pricing $3,000–$4,500+ (June 2026) after NVIDIA raised board prices $300 effective May 13, 2026. RTX 5080 MSRP $999, street ~$1,400–$1,800. RX 9070 XT: $600–$700 (relatively insulated, uses less GDDR7 than 5090). GDDR7 chip costs have surged several hundred percent year-over-year.

items.name: NVMe Gen4 SSD 2TB — Price Surge and Current Bands
items.specs: PCIe 4.0 x4 M.2 2280; seq read 7,000–7,400 MB/s; representative SKUs: Samsung 990 Pro 2TB, WD Black SN850X 2TB, Crucial T700 2TB
items.ai_relevance: Primary model weights storage for homelab AI nodes. 2TB is minimum practical for storing multiple GGUF model families (70B Q4 ~40GB each; 10–15 models = 400–600GB). Gen4 vs Gen5 difference is negligible for model loading latency vs. inference time.
items.compatibility: Requires M.2 slot with PCIe 4.0 support (Ryzen 5000+ AM4, all AM5, Intel 12th gen+). Gen4 drives in PCIe 3.0 slots throttle to ~3,500 MB/s — still functional. Samsung 990 Pro uses Samsung V-NAND; WD SN850X uses WD's own 112L TLC. Both widely supported on Linux (no driver issues), Windows 10/11. No CUDA/ROCm dependency.
items.price_band_usd: $325–$390 (June 2026). Samsung 990 Pro 2TB: $388 on Newegg. WD Black SN850X 2TB w/heatsink: $325 on Newegg. Was approximately $100–$145 in late 2024/early 2025 (historical Slickdeals data). Represents ~2.5–3x increase.

items.name: NVMe Gen5 SSD 2TB — Premium Tier (Samsung 9100 Pro / WD SN8100)
items.specs: PCIe 5.0 x4 M.2 2280; Samsung 9100 Pro: seq read 14,800 MB/s, seq write 13,400 MB/s; WD SN8100: ~14,500 MB/s read; 2TB capacity
items.ai_relevance: Marginally faster model load times vs. Gen4 for large GGUF files; useful for active model-switching workflows. For most inference tasks, compute is the bottleneck not storage bandwidth. Justified mainly for training data pipelines or high-frequency model swaps.
items.compatibility: Requires PCIe 5.0 M.2 slot (Intel 13th gen+, AMD AM5 with X670E chipset). High heat output — requires heatsink. No OS-level incompatibilities on Windows 11 or Linux 6.x. Thermal throttling observed on some builds without dedicated cooling.
items.price_band_usd: $478–$530 (June 2026 for 2TB). Samsung 9100 Pro 2TB: $478 per dropreference April 2026 data. WD Black SN8100 2TB: $285 (1TB) suggesting ~$500–$550 for 2TB. Was $189 in low-2025 for comparable 2TB Gen5 — representing ~+153% increase.

items.name: NVMe SSD 4TB — Shortage Bottleneck Tier
items.specs: 4TB M.2 NVMe; representative SKUs: Samsung 990 Pro 4TB, Crucial T705 4TB, WD SN850X 4TB; seq read 7,000+ MB/s (Gen4) or 12,000+ (Gen5)
items.ai_relevance: Enables storing an entire model zoo locally (all major 7B–70B families + fine-tunes); eliminates swapping to HDD for homelab inference servers; critical for RAG pipelines with large document corpora
items.compatibility: Same platform requirements as 2TB Gen4/Gen5 counterparts. 4TB drives have historically shown longer write latency under sustained writes due to SLC cache exhaustion — relevant for training data ingestion. Framework blog explicitly noted '4TB stock outages and outrageous pricing on remaining units' as of Q1-Q2 2026.
items.price_band_usd: $450–$700+ (June 2026, highly volatile with frequent stockouts). DropReference confirmed stockouts common. BuyPerUnit March 2026 data shows 4TB at higher $/GB premium than 2TB. Was $200–$250 in mid-2025. Accurate current price uncertain — verify before purchase.

items.name: Gartner 'Memflation' Forecast — Shortage Duration and 2027 Outlook
items.specs: Gartner February 2026 press release: 80% DRAM price inflation + 202% NAND flash inflation forecast for full-year 2026; coined term 'memflation'; PC prices projected +17%, smartphone prices +13%; global PC shipments forecast -10.4%
items.ai_relevance: Defines the macro environment for all AI hardware purchasing decisions. Sub-$500 consumer PCs forecast to disappear by 2028 per Gartner. Direct implication: AI workstations and homelab nodes built today face a 2–3 year window of structurally elevated DRAM/NAND costs.
items.compatibility: N/A — analyst forecast item. Gartner's February 26, 2026 press release is the primary authoritative source. TrendForce February 2, 2026 corroborates: DRAM contract prices expected to double in Q1 2026 QoQ, calling it 'record high QoQ increases across all product categories.'
items.price_band_usd: N/A (forecast, not product). Relief not expected before late 2027 per Gartner, TrendForce, and Wccftech consensus. Korea Economic Daily (Oct 2025) cited analysis that shortage could extend 3–4 years (to 2028–2029).

items.name: Root Cause: HBM4 / AI Wafer Diversion from Consumer DRAM
items.specs: AI workloads consuming ~20% of global DRAM wafer capacity in 2026 (TrendForce); 1GB HBM = 4GB standard DRAM in wafer area; Samsung began HBM4 mass production early 2026, pulling wafer starts from DDR5 lines; Samsung wafer output: 4.9M (2025) → 4.7M (2026); SK Hynix: 1.9M → 1.7M
items.ai_relevance: The structural reason AI hardware remains expensive: every HBM chip built for an H100/H200/B200 accelerator consumes wafer capacity that would otherwise produce ~4x the DDR5 for consumer use. This is a multi-year constraint, not a transient spike.
items.compatibility: N/A — market analysis. NVIDIA Grace Superchip uses up to 960GB LPDDR5X per node, further straining a memory segment (LPDDR5X) previously reserved for mobile, now competing with AI server demand. Samsung Q1 2026 profits: +755% YoY, 95% from memory — suppliers have no incentive to rebalance toward consumer.
items.price_band_usd: N/A — structural context. New Micron Idaho facility won't deliver substantial DRAM output until 2028. New NAND fabs from Samsung/Micron/SK Hynix reach meaningful capacity late 2027 at earliest. Chinese YMTC/CXMT expanding but full capacity not before 2027–2028.

items.name: CXMT Chinese DDR5 — Budget Alternative for Homelab
items.specs: CXMT (ChangXin Memory Technologies) produces DDR5-6000 to DDR5-8000 and LPDDR5X-10667; also DDR4; 32GB server RDIMM reported at $138 vs. $239+ for Samsung equivalent; consumer kit pricing 7–20% below Tier-1
items.ai_relevance: Only viable path to cost-effective memory expansion in 2026 for homelab/AI builds on a budget. CXMT DDR5 benchmarks show comparable performance to Samsung/SK Hynix in memory-bound tasks including LLM inference per dasroot.net analysis. Higher error rate under extended stress testing noted but within consumer-acceptable thresholds.
items.compatibility: Compatible with mainstream Intel LGA1700/1851 and AMD AM5 platforms per CXMT spec sheets. Limited US retail availability — primarily via Alibaba/direct Chinese distributors. Potential US import restrictions flagged as a risk by mid-2026 policy analysis. XMP/EXPO profiles may be fewer or less stable than Tier-1. Not recommended for ECC-critical workloads.
items.price_band_usd: $400–$550 for 32GB DDR5-6000 kit (vs. $500–$620 Tier-1 equivalent June 2026); 7–10% cheaper per June 2026 dasroot.net analysis. CXMT 32GB server RDIMM: $138 vs. Samsung $239 per aihola.com. Pricing volatile — recommend verifying at purchase time.

items.name: DDR4 Server RDIMM — Homelab Opportunity Tier
items.specs: DDR4-3200 ECC RDIMM, 32GB per DIMM; used in Intel Xeon (Cascade/Ice/Sapphire Lake) and AMD EPYC (Rome/Milan/Genoa-X) platforms; older-gen but supported in large used server market
items.ai_relevance: Used Intel/AMD server boards with 8–16 DIMM slots can support 256–512GB DDR4 RDIMM at dramatically lower cost than new DDR5 builds. Relevant for CPU-based inference of large models via llama.cpp, exllama, or vLLM CPU offload where bandwidth (not speed) matters most.
items.compatibility: DDR4 RDIMM requires registered (buffered) motherboard — incompatible with consumer AM4/LGA1700 platforms. Primarily targets Supermicro/Dell/HP server boards. Linux-first (ECC monitoring via edac-utils); Windows Server also supported. Not compatible with DDR5 or consumer DDR4 UDIMM slots.
items.price_band_usd: DDR4 RDIMM has seen less extreme inflation than DDR5 and is available cheaply in used/refurb channels. 32GB DDR4-3200 ECC RDIMM: $40–$90 used (enterprise surplus). New retail pricing has risen but used market remains insulated. Contrast: 256GB DDR4 new kit now reported at $3,000+ per Wccftech, but used server RAM is far cheaper.

items.name: Lock-Memory-at-Purchase Strategy — Practical Guidance
items.specs: N/A — purchasing guidance. Consensus across Gartner, TrendForce, Avnet, dropreference, bottleneckpc, techtimes: Q2-Q3 2026 prices still rising; no reversion to 2025 pricing before late 2027. DDR5 memory cost $15.13/GB in June 2026 (+9.5% MoM per WhereIsMyRam). AI server demand will absorb ~70% of data center capacity by 2030.
items.ai_relevance: For AI homelab builders: every month of delay adds ~9–10% to DRAM costs. Locking in memory at build time protects against further escalation. The strategy is validated by the data: a 32GB kit that cost $100 in mid-2025 costs $375–$520 today. Framework explicitly advises customers to buy memory at system purchase rather than upgrading later.
items.compatibility: Strategy applies to all memory tiers: DDR5 desktop, DDR5 SODIMM (laptop/mini-PC), GDDR7 (buy GPU now vs. later), NVMe (buy storage at time of build). Exception: if use case is non-urgent capacity expansion only, Gartner notes 'modest recent softening as demand erodes from high costs' — some softening possible in H2 2026 if AI capex pauses. Chinese CXMT expansion may provide modest relief Q3-Q4 2026 but US tariff risk exists.
items.price_band_usd: Cost of waiting: ~$30–$50 more per 32GB DDR5 kit per month at current inflation rate. Cost of acting: pay today's price which is already 3.5–4x mid-2025. For a full AI workstation build (128GB DDR5 + 4TB NVMe + GPU), delta between buying now vs. waiting 6 months = estimated $300–$600 additional cost at current trajectory.

items.name: LPDDR5X — AI Server and Premium Laptop Segment Price Surge
items.specs: LPDDR5X up to 9,600 MT/s (consumer) to 10,667 MT/s (CXMT demo); soldered on Apple Silicon (M4 Pro/Max/Ultra), Qualcomm Snapdragon X Elite, and NVIDIA Grace/Vera Superchips; not user-upgradeable in most implementations
items.ai_relevance: Apple M4 Max (128GB unified memory) and M4 Ultra (192GB) are the most VRAM-dense consumer AI inference platforms available without datacenter hardware. LPDDR5X bandwidth (800+ GB/s on M4 Max) enables 70B model inference at 20+ tokens/sec. NVIDIA Grace uses 960GB LPDDR5X per node for AI server inference.
items.compatibility: Not user-upgradeable on Apple Silicon or Qualcomm platforms — must be spec'd at purchase. Apple M4 Max 128GB config recommended for serious local LLM work; price now elevated due to LPDDR5X shortage. Tom's Hardware confirmed LPDDR5X prices could double in 2026 due to NVIDIA Grace demand creating a 'seismic shift' in that memory segment.
items.price_band_usd: Apple MacBook Pro M4 Max 128GB: ~$3,999–$4,499 (base 14-inch, 128GB config) as of June 2026. Premium vs. 36GB baseline config has widened due to LPDDR5X cost inflation. Mac Studio M4 Ultra 192GB: $5,999+. These prices are elevated vs. 2025 and unlikely to drop significantly before late 2027.

summary:
The 2026 DRAM/GDDR7/NAND shortage is a multi-year structural event, not a transient spike. The core driver is AI datacenter buildout diverting wafer capacity from consumer memory to HBM4 (for NVIDIA/AMD AI accelerators) and LPDDR5X (for NVIDIA Grace/Vera servers). Every GB of HBM consumes ~4GB of equivalent DRAM wafer area, pulling roughly 20% of global DRAM production away from consumer DDR5.

Key price trajectory (US market): DDR5 32GB kits went from $80–$133 in mid-2025 to $375–$520 by June 2026 — a 3.5–4x increase in under a year. 64GB DDR5 kits now run $390–$970 (volatile). 128GB workstation kits hit $1,459 at Newegg. NVMe SSDs have surged ~115% on average — a 2TB Samsung 990 Pro now costs $389 vs. ~$130 in late 2024. Gen5 2TB (Samsung 9100 Pro) is $478, up from $189 at 2025 lows. 4TB NVMe frequently out of stock.

GDDR7 scarcity is inflating GPU prices directly: NVIDIA raised RTX 5090 board prices $300 effective May 13, 2026, with street pricing now $3,000–$4,500+ vs. $1,999 MSRP. VRAM now constitutes 80%+ of GPU BOM.

Gartner forecasts 130% combined memory cost surge by end of 2026, coining "memflation." TrendForce called Q1 2026 QoQ increases "record highs across all product categories." Relief consensus: meaningful pricing normalization not before late 2027; some analysts extend to 2028–2029. New western fab capacity (Micron Idaho) not online until 2028. Chinese CXMT expansion may offer modest relief in Q3–Q4 2026 but US tariff risk is real.

Practical "lock memory at purchase" strategy is validated: DDR5 costs ~$15.13/GB in June 2026, +9.5% MoM. For AI homelab builders, the delta between buying now vs. waiting 6 months on a full build (128GB DDR5 + 4TB NVMe + GPU) is estimated at $300–$600 additional cost. Buy memory at system-build time and spec to ceiling rather than upgrading later. The one exception: non-urgent capacity upgrades may see modest softening if AI capex slows or CXMT floods Q3 2026 supply.

items.name: Dell Precision T7920 Tower (refurbished)
items.specs: Dual Intel Xeon Gold/Platinum (up to Xeon Platinum 8160, 48 cores/96 threads total); up to 256GB+ DDR4 ECC RDIMM (8 DIMM slots per CPU, 16 total); 3x PCIe 3.0 x16 + 2x PCIe 3.0 x8 slots; up to 1350W PSU; tool-free tower chassis; iDRAC-equivalent Dell management firmware
items.ai_relevance: Excellent GPU host for 1-2 full-length double-wide cards (RTX 3080/3090, A100 PCIe, L40S). 192-256GB RAM capacity handles large model context without GPU offload. Dual-socket Xeon Gold 6154 configs (36 cores) provide strong CPU-side pre/post-processing. Good for local LLM inference (13B-70B quantized), fine-tuning, and multi-GPU dev workloads. PCSP sells pre-configured 'AI & Machine Learning Workstation' SKUs with RTX 3080.
items.compatibility: PCIe 3.0 only — no PCIe 4.0, so A100 PCIe runs at PCIe 3.0 speeds (bandwidth capped at ~32 GB/s, not 64 GB/s, acceptable for inference). Windows 10/11 Pro and Linux both work natively. CUDA 12.x fully supported with appropriate GPU. iDRAC 9 on these units is reliable. Watch out: original GPUs are often Quadro P-series; verify PSU wattage before swapping in a 350W+ card. Some refurb units ship without GPU — budget accordingly.
items.price_band_usd: $800-$2,800 depending on CPU tier, RAM, and included GPU; PCSP lists 2x Xeon Platinum 8160 / 96GB / Quadro P2000 at ~$2,777; bare/no-GPU configs start lower

items.name: HP Z8 G4 Tower (refurbished)
items.specs: Dual Intel Xeon Scalable (Sky Lake/Cascade Lake, up to Xeon Gold 6143 36-core total); up to 384GB DDR4 ECC RDIMM; 6x PCIe 3.0 slots (up to 4x full-length GPU support); dual 1125W redundant PSU option; Z Turbo Drive NVMe bays; tool-free chassis
items.ai_relevance: HP's most GPU-dense workstation of its generation — up to 4 double-wide GPUs in correct config. 384GB RAM headroom is notable for large-model CPU offload (llama.cpp, ExLlamaV2). Suited for 2-4x RTX 3090/A6000 Ada multi-GPU inference clusters or a single A100 PCIe. HP specifically markets refurb Z8 G4 as AI/ML-capable. Amazon Renewed listings often include dual Quadro P5000 16GB configs.
items.compatibility: PCIe 3.0 only. Some GPU slots share bandwidth — verify slot topology before adding cards beyond two. Third-party PCIe 3.0 x16 cards (RTX 3090, A100 PCIe) work. HP iLO (Integrated Lights-Out) is strong for remote management. Linux driver support excellent. Watch: HP OEM fans may throttle loudly with non-HP GPUs triggering thermal alerts — BIOS fan control override sometimes needed.
items.price_band_usd: $1,000-$3,500 depending on CPU/RAM config; high-end Amazon Renewed (2x Xeon Gold 6143, 256GB, 2x Quadro P5000) ~$2,000-$2,500; base dual-Silver configs ~$1,000-$1,500

items.name: Lenovo ThinkStation P920 (refurbished)
items.specs: Dual Intel Xeon Scalable (up to Xeon Platinum 8168, 48 cores/96 threads); up to 1TB DDR4 ECC LRDIMM across 16 slots; 4x full-length PCIe 3.0 x16 GPU slots; 1400W PSU; Lenovo XClarity remote management; tool-free chassis with EMC shielding
items.ai_relevance: Lenovo's flagship dual-Xeon tower; 1TB RAM ceiling is the highest of the three major workstation platforms and relevant for extreme CPU-side LLM inference (70B+ unquantized). 4-GPU slot layout suits multi-GPU fine-tuning. PCSP sells pre-configured AI/ML SKUs with RTX 3090 24GB. The P920 is the quietest of the three in typical workloads due to larger fans spinning slower.
items.compatibility: PCIe 3.0. Lenovo proprietary power connectors on some GPU risers — verify compatibility when swapping in datacenter GPUs (A100 PCIe uses standard 8-pin/16-pin, generally fine). XClarity management requires separate license for full feature set. Windows 11 and Linux fully supported. Newegg lists refurbished P920 units with 384GB DDR4.
items.price_band_usd: $1,200-$4,000; example: 2x Xeon Gold 6154 / 384GB / Quadro P1000 on Newegg refurbished ~$1,400-$1,800; RTX 3090 AI/ML SKUs from PCSP ~$3,500+

items.name: Dell PowerEdge R750 (used/refurbished rack server)
items.specs: 2U dual-socket; Intel Xeon Ice Lake SP (3rd Gen Scalable, up to 40 cores/socket); up to 32 DIMM slots / 8TB DDR4 ECC; PCIe 4.0; up to 24x 2.5" NVMe bays; iDRAC9 remote management; redundant PSU; GPU support via riser (up to 3x double-wide PCIe 4.0 GPUs in GPU config)
items.ai_relevance: The R750 is the most current-gen used rack server available at meaningful discounts in 2026 (just entering secondary market from 3-year enterprise leases). PCIe 4.0 doubles GPU bandwidth vs R740 — critical for A100 PCIe peak throughput. Ideal for multi-tenant AI inference, Kubernetes GPU nodes, or a dense LLM server. iDRAC9 Enterprise enables full headless management. NVMe density suits fast model weight loading.
items.compatibility: Requires PCIe 4.0 risers for GPU — verify riser part number when buying without GPU. Ice Lake Xeon = CUDA 12.x, ROCm 5.x+ compatible with appropriate GPU. Linux (Ubuntu 22.04/24.04) preferred for AI stack. iDRAC9 is mature and well-supported. Watch: R750 is loud (40mm fans at high RPM) — not suitable for quiet home office without separate server room.
items.price_band_usd: $2,500-$8,000 for base configs without GPU; eBay and NewServerLife list R750 configs from ~$2,500 (minimal RAM) to $6,000+ (dual Gold 6338, 512GB RAM)

items.name: Dell PowerEdge R740 / HPE DL380 Gen10 (used rack server — value tier)
items.specs: 2U dual-socket; Intel Xeon Cascade Lake SP (2nd Gen, up to 28 cores/socket, 56 cores total); up to 3TB DDR4 ECC; PCIe 3.0; 2x 750-1100W PSU; iDRAC9/iLO5 management; up to 3x GPU risers (double-wide); heavy supply on secondary market
items.ai_relevance: The workhorse of 2026 homelab AI servers — high supply drives prices low. 56 CPU cores plus expandable ECC RAM makes an excellent Proxmox/KVM hypervisor for AI dev VMs, RAG pipeline servers, or CPU inference of 7B-13B models. Many homelabbers pair these with a used PCIe A100 or L40S. DDR4 ECC is dirt cheap in 2026 ($60 for 64GB used). R740 eBay listings span $655 to $11,856 depending on spec.
items.compatibility: PCIe 3.0 only. Standard PCIe cards work but GPU bandwidth is half of PCIe 4.0 servers. iDRAC9 on R740 is rock-solid; HPE iLO5 also excellent. Some R740 configs shipped with non-GPU risers — GPU riser kits sold separately. Loud in rack (65-75 dB typical). Enterprise Linux distros (RHEL, Ubuntu Server) well supported. Note: Cascade Lake has Meltdown/Spectre mitigations applied — small CPU overhead.
items.price_band_usd: $655-$4,000; common sweet spot: dual Xeon Gold 6226R (32 cores total), 256GB DDR4, dual PSU, no GPU ~$1,200-$2,000 on eBay

items.name: HPE ProLiant DL385 Gen10 Plus v2 / Dell R7525 (AMD EPYC rack server)
items.specs: 2U dual-socket AMD SP3 (EPYC 7002/7003 Milan, up to 64 cores/socket = 128 cores total); 32 DIMM slots / 4TB DDR4 ECC; PCIe 4.0 (v2 / R7525); 128 PCIe lanes per socket; GPU riser support up to 3x double-wide; 800W+ redundant PSU; iLO5 / iDRAC9
items.ai_relevance: Best core-per-dollar value in the used enterprise market as of 2026. 128 cores total for the price of a Xeon system with 56 cores. PCIe 4.0 + 128 PCIe lanes per socket enables direct GPU attachment without PCIe switch bottlenecks — ideal for 2-4 GPU AI training nodes. 4TB ECC RAM ceiling covers the largest CPU-inference workloads. EPYC Rome/Milan single-socket already outperforms dual Xeon in most ML pipeline tasks. Recommended by NewServerLife as the go-to EPYC AI/GPU server.
items.compatibility: CRITICAL: DL385 Gen10 (without v2) only supports EPYC 7001/7002, NOT 7003 (Milan). Only Gen10 Plus v2 / Dell R7525 supports Milan. Verify CPU generation before purchase. ROCm (for AMD GPU pairing) and CUDA both supported. Standard PCIe 4.0 GPUs (A100, L40S, RTX 3090) work. Some HPE models require HPE-branded NVMe for full firmware support. Dell R7525 starting from ~$6,847 refurbished at NewServerLife.
items.price_band_usd: DL385 Gen10 Plus from ~$1,504 (low-RAM); DL385 Gen10 Plus v2 higher; Dell R7525 from ~$6,847; HPE DL385 Gen10 on Newegg at $4,481 (7262 CPU / 16GB) to $15,014 (7702 / 32GB) new/refurb

items.name: NVIDIA Tesla V100 PCIe 16GB / 32GB (used datacenter GPU)
items.specs: Volta architecture (sm_70); 5120 CUDA cores; 640 Tensor Cores (1st gen); 900 GB/s HBM2 bandwidth (32GB) / 900 GB/s (16GB); 300W TDP PCIe; NVLink support on SXM2 variant only; 32GB model: 7.8 TFLOPS FP32, ~15.7 TFLOPS FP16; passive cooled (requires server chassis airflow)
items.ai_relevance: Budget entry point for datacenter-class AI. 32GB VRAM fits 13B parameter models at FP16 or 30B+ at 4-bit. Verified real-world performance: 65-138 tokens/sec on 24-35B quantized models via Ollama (NVLink dual-V100 build). V100 SXM2 NVLink pairs (dual 32GB = 64GB unified pool via NVLink 300 GB/s) are a popular homelab hack. CUDA 12 support ends at driver 580 — last officially supported version. 2026-2027 end-of-life for commercial support.
items.compatibility: sm_70 (Volta) — CUDA 12.x compatible but PyTorch 2.x drops Volta support in upcoming releases (check before purchasing for training). PCIe variant plugs into any PCIe 3.0/4.0 x16 slot. SXM2 variant REQUIRES NVLink adapter board (dual-card TNS-2SXM2-4P54 ~$230-380 on eBay, quad YMZX-4GPU-Q1 ~$400-800 via Taobao). Passive cooling means it MUST have forced airflow — will thermal-throttle in open air or desktop cases. Dell iDRAC may ramp fans to max with PCIe temp sensors from V100 SXM on custom boards (workaround: use non-Dell Threadripper host).
items.price_band_usd: PCIe 16GB: $400-900; PCIe 32GB: $700-1,500; SXM2 32GB: $800-1,200 (higher due to NVLink capability); complete dual-SXM2 NVLink homelab build achieved for ~$2,300-3,000 all-in

items.name: NVIDIA A100 40GB PCIe (used datacenter GPU)
items.specs: Ampere architecture (sm_80); 6912 CUDA cores; 432 3rd-gen Tensor Cores; 1.6 TB/s HBM2e bandwidth; 40GB HBM2e; 300W TDP PCIe; PCIe 4.0 x16; supports MIG (Multi-Instance GPU, 7 instances); BF16 supported natively
items.ai_relevance: The most capable GPU realistically achievable in a standard homelab PCIe slot. 40GB VRAM fits 30B models at FP16, 70B at 4-bit. MIG partitioning allows multiple isolated inference instances. BF16 support is important for modern fine-tuning (LoRA/QLoRA). CUDA sm_80 is fully supported by PyTorch 2.x, vLLM, llama.cpp CUDA, and all 2026 inference frameworks. Good for: LLM inference up to 70B, LoRA fine-tuning 7B-30B, embedding generation at scale. 2026 is described as 'last window of meaningful ROI' before Blackwell obsolescence accelerates.
items.compatibility: PCIe 4.0 — runs at PCIe 3.0 bandwidth if host is Gen3 (still functional, not optimal). Passive cooling only — requires server airflow or active cooling bracket. Standard PCIe 8-pin/16-pin power. IMPORTANT: A100 PCIe and A100 SXM4 are NOT interchangeable physically or electrically. Used PCIe 40GB units sometimes converted from SXM4 — verify form factor. Some converted SXM-to-PCIe adapters have wiring issues with sense pins (documented NVIDIA forum reports). Buy from reputable vendor with documented PCIe provenance.
items.price_band_usd: $2,000-$4,500 used (40GB PCIe); 80GB PCIe used: $4,800-$9,000; market declining ~10-15% through 2026 as Blackwell supply increases

items.name: NVIDIA H100 PCIe 80GB (used/refurbished datacenter GPU)
items.specs: Hopper architecture (sm_90); 14,592 CUDA cores; 456 4th-gen Tensor Cores; 2 TB/s HBM2e bandwidth; 80GB HBM2e; 350W TDP PCIe; PCIe 5.0 x16 (backwards compatible); NVLink 4.0 (PCIe variant = limited NVLink, not full SXM5 NVSwitch fabric); FP8 support
items.ai_relevance: Highest-performance single datacenter GPU realistically purchasable for a well-funded homelab. FP8 training support (2x throughput vs FP16 on compatible frameworks). 80GB fits 70B models at FP16 or 405B at 4-bit. Flash Attention 2/3 optimized on Hopper. For inference: best single-card token throughput available. For small-cluster training: PCIe variant lacks full NVSwitch (3-hop NVLink) but still outperforms A100 significantly. Suitable for serious inference serving, multi-LoRA fine-tuning, and RAG pipeline acceleration.
items.compatibility: PCIe 5.0 — will run in PCIe 4.0 or 3.0 slots at reduced bandwidth (still functional for most workloads). H100 SXM5 is NOT compatible with standard PCIe slots and requires HGX/DGX baseboard — avoid SXM5 for homelab unless you have an HGX platform. CUDA 12.0+ required for full Hopper feature set. Linux preferred (NVIDIA driver 525+). Warranty does NOT transfer on secondary market. 60-day burn-in from certified refurbishers (ALTA, Compute Exchange) recommended.
items.price_band_usd: Used (non-refurbished): $15,000-$28,000; Refurbished (certified): $21,000-$34,000; New: $25,000-$40,000. SXM5 commands premium over PCIe in all tiers. Effective savings vs new shrink to ~9% after warranty gap and elevated 2-4.5% annual failure rate.

items.name: Used DDR4 ECC Registered Server RAM (component)
items.specs: DDR4 ECC RDIMM / LRDIMM; typically 16GB, 32GB, or 64GB sticks; speeds 2400-3200 MHz; quad-rank for high-density (4Rx4); compatible with all Xeon Scalable and EPYC SP3 platforms
items.ai_relevance: Critical enabler of cost-effective used enterprise AI builds. 64GB DDR4 ECC used available for under $60 — vs $300+ for 32GB consumer DDR5. Maxing RAM (128-256GB) on a used server/workstation allows full CPU-side LLM inference (llama.cpp, Ollama) of 30-70B models without GPU, or hybrid GPU+CPU offload for larger models. A used R740 with 256GB ECC RAM costs roughly the same as a mid-range consumer CPU + DDR5 kit.
items.compatibility: Must match server platform: Dell PowerEdge R740/R750 use specific RDIMM configurations (check memory population rules — typically fill pairs per channel). LRDIMM required for capacities above 256GB on many platforms. Xeon Scalable: uses DDR4 ECC RDIMM; EPYC SP3: uses DDR4 ECC RDIMM (8 channels/socket). Consumer DDR4/DDR5 does NOT work in server motherboards. Always verify part number against server's QVL (Qualified Vendor List) for full speed support.
items.price_band_usd: 16GB DDR4 ECC RDIMM: $8-15; 32GB: $20-40; 64GB: $45-80; 192GB kit (6x32GB) for Dell C6420: ~$242 on eBay

items.name: Where to Buy: Trusted Secondary Market Vendors
items.specs: Key vendors: NewServerLife (custom-configured, stress-tested, warranty), PCSP / PC Server and Parts (Dell/HP/Lenovo workstations + servers, up to 5yr warranty, pre-configured AI/ML SKUs), ALTA Technologies (GPU-specialist: used A100/H100/V100, Supermicro servers), Compute Exchange (datacenter GPU specialist, refurbished H100/A100), SaveMyServer (up to 70% off, enterprise warranty), CXtec (40yr legacy, North America refurb), eBay (largest selection, lowest prices, highest risk), Amazon Renewed (tested, warranty, fast shipping, premium over eBay)
items.ai_relevance: Vendor selection directly impacts failure risk for GPU purchases. For A100/H100: prefer ALTA Technologies or Compute Exchange (burn-in documentation, provenance tracking). For servers: NewServerLife or PCSP offer configured systems with warranties. For budget builds: eBay with seller feedback >99% and 30-day return policy. Avoid auction-only listings for high-value GPUs without burn-in certs.
items.compatibility: eBay: verify seller return policy and condition description. Amazon Renewed: 90-day Amazon guarantee minimum. Specialized refurbishers (NewServerLife, PCSP): typically offer 1-3yr warranties and will configure to spec. For GPU-only purchases, request burn-in report and thermal history. NVIDIA OEM warranty never transfers on secondary market — any warranty is seller-provided.
items.price_band_usd: Prices vary; certified refurb commands 10-20% premium over eBay used; PCSP/NewServerLife typically 15-30% above eBay but includes warranty and testing

items.name: Key Pitfalls: Noise, Power, Thermals, and Hidden Costs
items.specs: Rack servers (R740/R750/DL385): 40mm fans at 7,000+ RPM = 65-75 dB; unsuitable for home office without dedicated server room. Power draw: 240W typical idle, spikes to 600W+ under GPU load. Monthly electricity at $0.12/kWh: $21-65/month per server at continuous load.
