Tower workstations (Z8 G4, T7920, P920): 50-60 dB, suitable for office. iDRAC/iLO out-of-band management licenses sometimes required for full feature sets (verify included). RAID controller passthrough (HBA mode) needed for ZFS — check for H730/H740P vs HBA330.
items.ai_relevance: Power and noise are the primary reasons enterprise homelabbers switch from rack servers to workstation towers for always-on AI inference. A tower workstation running 24/7 at 150-200W costs ~$13-17/month — vs $40-60/month for a loaded rack server. For occasional batch workloads, rack servers make sense. For always-on LLM inference, a T7920 or Z8 G4 with a single A100 PCIe is the practical choice. Mini PCs (ASUS NUC-equivalent) can undercut all of these for sub-7B inference.
items.compatibility: Thermal management: passive-cooled datacenter GPUs (A100, V100, L40S) require chassis airflow — do not install in consumer PC cases. Some enterprise servers with third-party GPUs trigger aggressive fan responses (Dell thermal override workaround: ipmitool raw command fan speed override). ECC RAM incompatible with consumer motherboards. Old iDRAC versions (iDRAC 7/8 on older servers) may lack modern SSL support for web UI — upgrade firmware first.
items.price_band_usd: Hidden costs to budget: iDRAC Enterprise license $50-150 if not included; rack + PDU + patch panel for rack servers $200-600; 10GbE NIC if not present $30-100 used; GPU power cables/risers $20-80; annual electricity $250-700/server

summary: The used enterprise compute market in mid-2026 offers strong value-per-dollar for small-lab and homelab AI/dev procurement, with three main tiers: (1) Dual-Xeon workstations (Dell T7920, HP Z8 G4, Lenovo P920) at $800-$3,500 are the best all-around homelab AI hosts — quiet enough for office use, PCIe x16 GPU slots, 128-384GB ECC RAM, and easy to configure; (2) Used rack servers (R740, DL385 Gen10, R7525) at $1,200-$8,000 maximize cores and RAM density but add 65-75 dB noise, high power draw, and rack infrastructure costs; (3) Used datacenter GPUs — V100 32GB PCIe ($700-1,500) is the budget entry for LLM inference with CUDA 12 (driver 580 is the last supported), A100 40GB PCIe ($2,000-4,500) is the current value sweet spot for serious work, and H100 PCIe ($15,000-28,000 used) is best-in-class but carry 2-4.5% annual failure rates with no NVIDIA warranty transfer. Key pitfalls: A100/H100 SXM variants cannot be used in standard PCIe slots; HPE DL385 Gen10 (non-v2) does not support EPYC 7003 Milan; passive-cooled datacenter GPUs require forced chassis airflow; DDR4 ECC RAM is cheap ($60 for 64GB) and should be maxed. Best vendors for GPU procurement: ALTA Technologies and Compute Exchange (burn-in documentation); for servers: NewServerLife and PCSP (configured, warranted). eBay has lowest prices but demands careful seller vetting.

items.name: Ollama
items.specs: No VRAM of its own; leverages host GPU. Supports CUDA v12/v13 backends (NVIDIA cc 5.0+, including sm_120/sm_121 Blackwell cc12.0/12.1). ROCm v7.1/v7.2 on Linux for RDNA2/RDNA3/RDNA4/Instinct. Metal on Apple Silicon M1-M4 Ultra. Experimental Vulkan backend for Intel Arc, older AMD, unsupported cards. CPU fallback always available.
items.ai_relevance: Best entry-point for consumer local AI: pull-and-run GGUF models, OpenAI-compatible REST API, automatic GPU layer splitting. Runs any model fitting in VRAM; larger models spill to RAM/CPU. Ideal for 7B-70B range on RTX 3090/4090/50xx or Apple Silicon. Blackwell sm_12.0/12.1 fully listed in supported compute table as of 2026.
items.compatibility: NVIDIA: requires driver 531+ Windows / 535+ Linux (cc 6.1+); driver 570+ for cc 5.0-6.2. AMD ROCm is Linux-only — no native Windows ROCm support; Vulkan fills the gap on Windows AMD/Intel. ROCm v7 required. Apple: Metal, all M-series. ARM64 Linux: Vulkan default backend. Known trap: AMD ROCm Windows users must use Vulkan path or WSL2.
items.price_band_usd: Free / open source

items.name: llama.cpp
items.specs: CPU (x86_64, aarch64, RISC-V), CUDA 12.x and 13.x (NVIDIA), ROCm/HIP 7.2 (Ubuntu x64), Metal (Apple Silicon), Vulkan (cross-platform x86 and arm64), Intel SYCL/oneAPI. Prebuilt Windows binaries b9196+ ship CUDA 12.4, CUDA 13.1, Vulkan, HIP, and SYCL variants simultaneously.
items.ai_relevance: The universal GGUF runtime underpinning Ollama, LM Studio, Jan, and many wrappers. Runs 1.5B-405B+ via GGUF quantization (Q2_K through Q8_0, IQ variants). Best consumer-grade throughput for NVIDIA and Apple. On Blackwell RTX 50xx (sm_120), CUDA 12.8 builds are recommended — CUDA 13.1 builds trigger MMQ kernel segfaults on some sm_120 configs. On GB10 (sm_121), Vulkan or CUDA 12.9 paths are used.
items.compatibility: CUDA 12.8 is the recommended build for Blackwell sm_120; CUDA 13.1 prebuilts exist but MMQ crash is a known trap. Vulkan is the default backend on aarch64 Linux (Qualcomm X Elite, GB10). ROCm kernel is functional but significantly slower than CUDA equivalent — community-maintained. Apple Metal: full support M1-M4 Ultra. MXFP4 kernel compilation on sm_120 is broken in some commit ranges (issue #19662); roll back pre-MXFP4 if needed.
items.price_band_usd: Free / open source (MIT)

items.name: vLLM
items.specs: CUDA 12.x primary (CUDA 12.8/12.9 recommended); CUDA 13 system support experimental. ROCm 6.3+ / 7.0 / 7.2.1 on Linux. No native Windows — WSL2 required. No official Apple Metal backend (community vllm-metal plugin available via Docker Model Runner). Supports AWQ, GPTQ, FP8 (H100), NVFP4 (Blackwell). OpenAI-compatible /v1 API.
items.ai_relevance: De facto production serving stack for data-center and high-end workstation inference. PagedAttention enables high-throughput multi-user serving; continuous batching, chunked prefill, prefix caching, tensor parallelism. Recommended for H100/A100/RTX 40xx/50xx serving 13B-405B+ models at scale. On Blackwell GB200/B200 (sm_120) it is the primary NVFP4/FP8 path.
items.compatibility: Critical trap: NO stable cu130 aarch64 wheels on PyPI as of mid-2026. GB10/DGX Spark (sm_121 aarch64) requires build-from-source targeting sm_120 with CUDA 12.9 overrides, patching cutlass_moe_mm and cutlass_scaled_mm backends, and pinning torchvision 0.24.1. PyTorch cu124 index has x86_64 only — no ARM64 CUDA wheels. sm_120/sm_121 are binary compatible so sm_120 kernels run on sm_121. vLLM 0.17+ fixed float8 padding bug on sm_121. Windows: WSL2 only. ROCm: Linux only, prebuilt wheels for ROCm 7.0 and 7.2.1 available. Issue #36821 tracks official sm_121 aarch64 support.
items.price_band_usd: Free / open source (Apache 2.0)

items.name: ONNX Runtime / Windows ML
items.specs: Execution Providers: CUDA (NVIDIA), ROCm/MIGraphX (AMD), DirectML (Windows GPU/NPU cross-vendor), TensorRT, CoreML (Apple), OpenVINO (Intel), QNN (Qualcomm Hexagon NPU on Snapdragon X Elite). Windows ML is the Microsoft-maintained Windows-bundled copy. Supports x64 and ARM64 (DirectML 1.15.4+ for ARM64). CPU always available.
items.ai_relevance: Best cross-vendor inference path for Windows AI PCs, Copilot+ PCs, and edge deployment. DirectML enables GPU-accelerated inference on any D3D12 GPU without CUDA/ROCm. QNN EP targets Qualcomm Hexagon NPU on Snapdragon X Elite (up to 45 TOPS). Useful for ONNX-format models (Phi-3, Llama-ONNX, Whisper, diffusion UNets). WinML auto-converts to BF16 for NPU execution and auto-discovers EPs in 2026.
items.compatibility: DirectML is in sustained engineering — new Windows projects should target WinML instead (it wraps ONNX Runtime). ROCm EP: Linux only via separate wheel. CUDA EP: standard onnxruntime-gpu package. ARM64: DirectML 1.15.4+ required. Known trap: onnxruntime-gpu ships only CUDA+TensorRT EPs; DirectML requires separate onnxruntime-directml package. ROCm wheel includes MIGraphX EP. No Vulkan EP natively.
items.price_band_usd: Free / open source (MIT)

items.name: TGI (Text Generation Inference)
items.kind: HuggingFace LLM serving engine (now in maintenance mode)
items.specs: CUDA (primary, updated to Torch 2.7 / CUDA 12.8 in final releases). ROCm (AMD Instinct, maintained in same repo). Intel Gaudi (forked repo, periodically synced). Intel GPU, AWS Trainium/Inferentia, Google TPU. OpenAI-compatible API, continuous batching, flash-attention, quantization (GPTQ, AWQ, FP8).
items.ai_relevance: Was the leading production serving stack for HuggingFace Hub models 2022-2025. Pioneered continuous batching and flash-attention integration. Now superseded: the upstream GitHub repo was archived read-only on March 21, 2026. HuggingFace officially redirects users to vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp, and MLX. IBM maintains an active fork. Existing deployments remain valid but no new features.
items.compatibility: Final releases target CUDA 12.8 / Torch 2.7. No CUDA 13 or sm_121 support added before archival. ROCm support on AMD Instinct (Linux) was maintained. Intel Gaudi support in separate fork. Trap: archived repo means no security patches or Blackwell optimizations. For new deployments, migrate to vLLM or SGLang.
items.price_band_usd: Free / open source (Apache 2.0); archived

items.name: ExLlamaV2
items.kind: fast GPU inference library / EXL2 quantization runtime
items.specs: NVIDIA CUDA only (primary); compiles Torch C++ extension at install time targeting the local GPU's compute capability. ROCm support exists via community fork (leonxia1018/exllamav2_Rocm) but is unoptimized vs CUDA. No Metal, no Vulkan, no CPU path. Requires CUDA-capable GPU (cc 6.0+ recommended). EXL2 format supports 2-8 bit mixed-precision quantization.
items.ai_relevance: Highest single-GPU tokens/sec for GGUF-alternative EXL2 quantized models on consumer NVIDIA cards (RTX 3090, 4090, 50xx). Particularly strong on 7B-34B models at 4-6 bpp. Used as the inference backend in text-generation-webui and koboldcpp. EXL2 allows mixing quantization levels per layer for any average bitrate between 2-8 bits. VRAM: ~8GB for 7B at 5bpp, ~24GB for 70B at 4bpp.
items.compatibility: NVIDIA-only for optimized performance. Compile-time extension means wheel must match local CUDA toolkit version — pre-built wheels on PyPI target specific CUDA versions. ROCm fork is functional but significantly slower (no custom kernels, falls back to ROCm HIP equivalents). No aarch64 support in the main repo. No Windows ROCm. Known trap: building on CUDA 13 systems requires patching setup.py CUDA arch detection. Blackwell (sm_120) works via runtime compilation if CUDA 12.8+ toolkit is installed.
items.price_band_usd: Free / open source (MIT)

items.name: MLX (Apple)
items.kind: Apple-native ML framework and inference stack
items.specs: Apple Silicon only (aarch64 + Metal GPU). Supports M1 through M4 Max / M4 Ultra (up to 512GB unified memory, 800GB/s+ bandwidth). Metal compute shaders with lazy evaluation and zero-copy unified memory. mlx-lm provides LLM inference with speculative decoding, KV cache management, AWQ/GPTQ-style quantization (4-bit, 8-bit). No CUDA, no ROCm, no x86 support.
items.ai_relevance: Best inference path on Apple Silicon — beats Ollama (which uses llama.cpp Metal) by 15-30% throughput and ~10% lower memory. True zero-copy: CPU/GPU/Neural Engine share the same memory pool, eliminating PCIe transfer overhead. M4 Ultra (512GB) can run Llama 3.1 405B at 4-bit with headroom. M4 Max (128GB, 546GB/s) approaches datacenter GPU bandwidth. Apple ML Research paper (arxiv 2601.19139) benchmarks MLX at scale on Apple Silicon.
items.compatibility: macOS only, Apple Silicon required (M1+). Rosetta/x86_64 Python is not supported — native arm64 Python 3.12 required. No Windows, no Linux, no NVIDIA. vllm-metal (community, Docker Model Runner) is a separate project bringing vLLM to Metal. MLX itself has no OpenAI-compatible server out of the box — use mlx-lm's built-in server or wrap with LiteLLM. Known trap: model weights must be converted to MLX format (mlx-lm provides converters); HuggingFace GGUF files require conversion.
items.price_band_usd: Free / open source (Apache 2.0)

items.name: TensorRT-LLM
items.specs: NVIDIA CUDA only. Supports Hopper (sm_90, H100/H200), Ada (sm_89, L40S, RTX 40xx), Ampere (sm_80/86, A100, A30, RTX 30xx), and Blackwell (sm_120 B200/GB200/GB300, sm_121 GB10/DGX Spark). NVFP4 kernels for Blackwell (requires TensorRT 10.9+ / CUDA 12.8+). CUDA 13 CUTLASS DSL in v1.3.x. FP8, INT8, INT4 quantization. Multi-GPU tensor/pipeline parallelism. FlashInfer NVFP4 MoE backend for sm_120/sm_121.
items.ai_relevance: Maximum throughput for NVIDIA datacenter inference — used in NVIDIA NIM, Triton Inference Server backends. Delivers 2-4x throughput over vanilla PyTorch via fused kernels, NVFP4 Blackwell GEMM, and in-flight batching. Required for enterprise B200/GB200/H100 cluster deployments. NVFP4 on Blackwell is the highest-performance quantization path available. Supports DeepSeek V3 block-scale FP8 on Blackwell.
items.compatibility: NVIDIA GPUs only — no AMD, no Apple, no Intel. pip install not supported for Blackwell (sm_120) on TRT-LLM 0.17; build from source via NGC 25.01 PyTorch container. sm_121 (GB10 DGX Spark): FP4 CUTLASS GEMM had a shared-memory overflow bug (issue #11368) from B200-sized tile configs being applied to smaller GB10; fixed in later 1.3.x RCs. SM120 dependency regression in 1.3.0rc2 (issue #11386) caused kernel occupancy warnings. For aarch64 GB10, build-from-source is the only validated path. CUDA 12.8 minimum for FP4; CUDA 13 for CuteDSL GEMM path.
items.price_band_usd: Free / open source (Apache 2.0); requires NVIDIA GPU

summary: As of June 2026, the local-AI inference stack landscape has consolidated around 4 primary use-case tiers. For consumer/developer ease-of-use: Ollama (wraps llama.cpp, supports CUDA 12/13/sm_121, ROCm 7, Metal, Vulkan) is the entry point. For raw GGUF performance: llama.cpp directly, with CUDA 12.8 recommended for Blackwell sm_120 — CUDA 13.1 triggers MMQ segfaults on some sm_120 configs, and Vulkan is the default on aarch64 Linux. For production multi-user serving: vLLM dominates on NVIDIA (x86_64 Linux), but has a critical gap — no stable cu130 aarch64 wheels, meaning GB10/DGX Spark (sm_121 aarch64) requires build-from-source with CUDA 12.9 override flags; the upstream TGI was archived read-only March 21 2026 in favor of vLLM/SGLang. For maximum NVIDIA throughput: TensorRT-LLM with NVFP4 on Blackwell B200/GB200, though sm_121 (GB10) still requires NGC container builds due to pip install limitations and FP4 tile-config bugs fixed only in late 1.3.x RCs. For Apple Silicon: MLX outperforms Ollama/Metal by 15-30% via true zero-copy unified memory; M4 Ultra (512GB) enables 405B+ parameter models locally. For cross-vendor Windows/NPU: ONNX Runtime with DirectML or WinML targets Qualcomm Hexagon NPU (Copilot+ PCs) and any D3D12 GPU. ExLlamaV2 remains the fastest single-GPU path for EXL2-quantized models on NVIDIA consumer hardware, with community-only ROCm support. The most significant cross-stack compatibility trap in 2026 is the CUDA 12 vs 13 split on Blackwell aarch64: nearly all ML wheels are cu12x, while Blackwell sm_121 systems ship CUDA 13 by default.

items.name: Proxmox VE
items.specs: Current release: VE 9.1 (April 2026), based on Debian 13 Trixie, Linux kernel 6.14.8, QEMU 10.0, LXC 6.0, ZFS 2.3. Supports KVM VMs and LXC containers on the same host. LXC idle overhead ~200–400 MB RAM per container. PCIe passthrough via VFIO; supports NVIDIA vGPU (driver v19.4+), Intel SR-IOV vGPU (Arc/Flex), AMD MxGPU. Web UI + REST API. OCI image support for LXC in 9.x. Clustered HA across multiple nodes. Free (AGPLv3); optional subscriptions for enterprise repo and SLA support.
items.price_band_usd: Free (OSS). Subscriptions: Community €120/CPU-socket/yr (~$130), Basic €370 (~$400), Standard €550 (~$600), Premium €1,100 (~$1,200) — per socket, not per developer seat.
items.ai_relevance: Ideal top-level orchestrator for a 3–4 dev shared-rig setup. Each developer or project can get an isolated VM or LXC container on the same bare-metal host. PCIe passthrough assigns a dedicated GPU to a VM (e.g., one developer gets the RTX 4090, another gets an Arc A770). SR-IOV/MxGPU slices one GPU across multiple VMs. LXC containers share the host kernel with near-zero overhead, suitable for running separate Ollama/vLLM inference daemons. Well-suited for 7B–70B+ quantized models when passthrough assigns sufficient VRAM per container. GPU cannot be simultaneously shared across passthrough VMs without SR-IOV-capable hardware.
items.compatibility: Linux only (Debian-based host). Guest: any OS via KVM. NVIDIA vGPU requires licensed NVIDIA drivers (vGPU Manager); bare passthrough is free. Intel Arc/Flex SR-IOV works on kernel 6.12+. AMD MxGPU requires Radeon Pro/Instinct cards. No ROCm vGPU slice support in standard configs — ROCm workloads best done via full GPU passthrough or bare-metal LXC. ARM/aarch64 host support is experimental in VE 9.x.

items.name: Docker + NVIDIA Container Toolkit
items.specs: Docker Engine 27.x (current as of 2026). NVIDIA Container Toolkit v1.17.3 stable (tested on Docker 27.5 + CUDA 12.8); v1.18.0-rc available. Registers 'nvidia' custom runtime with dockerd. GPU assignment via --gpus flag (by index or UUID) or NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES env var. Supports MIG (multi-instance GPU) slicing on A100/H100 series. Each container gets isolated CUDA userspace; multiple CUDA versions can coexist across containers on the same host. Docker Compose for multi-service stacks.
items.price_band_usd: Free (Docker CE + NVIDIA Container Toolkit both OSS). Docker Desktop license required on Windows/macOS for commercial use ($21/user/month or $25/user/month team plan); Linux Docker Engine is always free.
items.ai_relevance: The foundational layer for shared-GPU multi-developer labs. Each developer runs their own container image with pinned CUDA/Python/library versions — eliminates dependency conflicts. GPU sharing: containers can request --gpus all (all GPUs) or specific indices. For 3–4 devs on one rig: assign GPU 0 to dev-A's inference container, GPU 1 to dev-B's training container. Runs vLLM, Ollama, TGI, ComfyUI, Jupyter, etc. in isolated namespaces. Works at 7B–70B+ depending on available VRAM per container. NVIDIA Container Toolkit is required on Linux; Docker Desktop on Windows/macOS has built-in WSL2 GPU passthrough.
items.compatibility: Linux (primary target for NVIDIA CT). Windows via WSL2 + Docker Desktop (CUDA 12.x supported in WSL2). macOS: no NVIDIA; Metal via llama.cpp/MLX but NOT via NVIDIA CT. ROCm Docker images available from AMD (rocm/pytorch etc.) but ROCm CT is a separate install path. CUDA compute capabilities: any sm_60+ GPU works; sm_120 (RTX 50xx Blackwell) requires CUDA 13 containers. Wheel trap: PyTorch nightly needed for Blackwell sm_120 as of mid-2026.

items.name: k3s (Lightweight Kubernetes)
items.specs: k3s is Rancher/SUSE's certified Kubernetes distribution packaged as a single ~70 MB binary. Supports multi-node clusters (1 server + N agents). Integrates Traefik ingress, CoreDNS, local-path-provisioner, Helm controller out of the box. GPU scheduling via NVIDIA device plugin for Kubernetes (k8s.io/gpu-device-plugin); resource requests: nvidia.com/gpu: 1 in pod spec. Supports KubeSpawner for JupyterHub on k3s. Self-healing: pods restart on node failure. Resource requirements: ~512 MB RAM for server node.
items.price_band_usd: Free (Apache 2.0 OSS). No licensing cost.
items.ai_relevance: Step up from Docker Compose when the team needs self-healing, declarative state, and proper ingress for inference APIs. For 3–4 devs: each developer's inference service (Ollama, vLLM, TGI) runs as a Kubernetes Deployment with GPU resource requests; Kubernetes scheduler queues pods if GPUs are fully allocated. Enables multi-node: spread inference across two rigs with a shared model-weight PVC (backed by TrueNAS NFS). Zero-to-JupyterHub Helm chart deploys a multi-user notebook platform in ~30 min on k3s. Recommended threshold: once the team exceeds 2–3 concurrent GPU services or needs 24/7 uptime across reboots. Overkill for a single shared rig with 2 devs; right-sized for 3–4 devs or two rigs.
items.compatibility: Linux x86_64 and aarch64 (Raspberry Pi clusters, Jetson Orin). Minimal kernel requirement: 5.8+. Works on bare metal or VMs (including Proxmox VMs). Not natively Windows (requires WSL2 or VM). NVIDIA device plugin requires NVIDIA Container Toolkit pre-installed on agent nodes. AMD ROCm device plugin exists but is less mature. Incompatible with standard containerd without extra config for ROCm — NVIDIA path is well-documented.

items.name: Tailscale
items.specs: WireGuard-based mesh VPN. Peer-to-peer connections when possible; DERP relay servers as fallback. Devices addressed on a 100.x.x.x tailnet. MagicDNS assigns stable hostnames (e.g., gpu-rig.tailnet). ACL policies control which users/devices can reach which ports. Free Personal plan: up to 3 users, 100 devices. Standard: unlimited users at $8/user/month, up to 10 ACL groups. Premium: $18/user/month, SSO/SAML, 300 ACL groups, audit logs. Self-hosted alternative: Headscale (open source control server, Apache 2.0) — full feature parity for small teams, single tailnet.
items.price_band_usd: Free (Personal, up to 3 users). Standard: $8/user/month (~$32/month for 4 devs). Premium: $18/user/month (~$72/month for 4 devs). Headscale: free self-hosted.
items.ai_relevance: The network glue for a 'laptops-as-thin-clients, central rig does compute' topology. Each developer installs Tailscale on their laptop; the GPU rig is a Tailscale node. Developers SSH, VS Code Remote, or hit inference APIs (Ollama :11434, vLLM :8000) via the 100.x.x.x address from anywhere — no port forwarding, no DDNS. Works through NAT, CGNAT, hotel Wi-Fi. ACL tags (e.g., tag:gpu-rig) restrict which developer accounts can reach which GPU containers. Pairs directly with VS Code Remote Tunnels or SSH. Headscale is the right call if the team is privacy-sensitive or wants zero dependency on Tailscale's cloud control plane.
items.compatibility: Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android, tvOS. Tailscale client is open source (BSD). Headscale server: Linux (binary or Docker). No platform-specific GPU/AI compatibility issues — it is purely a network layer. Kernel WireGuard (Linux 5.6+) preferred; falls back to userspace WireGuard on older kernels.

items.name: JupyterHub
items.specs: JupyterHub manages authentication, spawns isolated single-user JupyterLab/Notebook servers, and provides a central hub. Current stable: 4.x series. Spawner backends: DockerSpawner (one container per user with configurable GPU limits via resource requests), KubeSpawner (one pod per user on Kubernetes with nvidia.com/gpu resource limits), SystemdSpawner (systemd cgroups). Supports PAM, OAuth, LDAP authentication. ProfileList spawner option lets users pick CPU/GPU/RAM profiles at login. Persistent user storage via volume mounts.
items.price_band_usd: Free (BSD 3-Clause OSS). Infrastructure costs only (the GPU server itself).
items.ai_relevance: Purpose-built for the '3–4 devs sharing a central GPU rig' scenario. Each developer authenticates and gets their own JupyterLab container/pod with assigned GPU resources — no SSH or container knowledge required. Profiles can offer e.g. 'CPU only', '1x GPU (8 GB VRAM)', '2x GPU' tiers. Users run model training, inference experiments, and data analysis in fully isolated Python environments. Works with any model size the assigned VRAM supports. Integrates directly with shared model weight storage on TrueNAS NFS mounts inside user containers. Zero-to-JupyterHub Helm chart on k3s is the recommended path for teams already running Kubernetes.
items.compatibility: Linux server (Ubuntu 22.04/24.04 most common). NVIDIA GPU support via DockerSpawner + NVIDIA Container Toolkit or KubeSpawner + device plugin. ROCm: works with AMD ROCm Docker images via DockerSpawner but less documented. macOS/Windows: JupyterHub server must run on Linux; clients are browser-based (any OS). Python 3.9+ required. Common wheel trap: user containers need matching CUDA toolkit to host driver — pin cuda-toolkit version in spawner image, do not use 'latest'.

items.name: VS Code Remote Development (SSH + Tunnels)
items.specs: Two modes: (1) Remote - SSH: VS Code connects over SSH to run extensions and language servers on the remote host. Standard OpenSSH on port 22. Full IntelliSense, debugger, terminal. (2) Remote - Tunnels: VS Code CLI runs on the remote machine; outbound tunnel to Microsoft Azure relay (no inbound firewall ports needed). Auth via GitHub or Microsoft account. Also works via vscode.dev in browser. Self-hosted alternative: code-server (open source, Coder) — runs VS Code in browser from your own server, no Microsoft cloud dependency. Coder platform adds team management, workspace quotas, and multi-user provisioning.
items.price_band_usd: VS Code Remote (SSH + Tunnels): free. code-server: free OSS. Coder platform: free for OSS self-hosted; Coder Cloud enterprise pricing custom.
items.ai_relevance: The thin-client access layer for the shared-rig topology: developers code on lightweight laptops while all computation (Python inference, model training, RAG pipelines) runs on the central GPU rig. SSH mode is best inside a Tailscale tailnet — developer SSHs to gpu-rig.tailnet. Tunnel mode works without Tailscale for developers who cannot install VPN clients. Full debugger + GPU profiler (Nsight, py-spy) work over SSH. For AI dev specifically: run inference inside a Docker container on the rig, VS Code Remote into that container via Dev Containers extension. code-server is preferred for air-gapped environments or when developers use Chromebooks/tablets.
items.compatibility: VS Code Remote SSH: Linux/macOS/Windows server, any OS client. Requires SSH server (OpenSSH). VS Code Tunnels: Linux/macOS/Windows server; outbound HTTPS to Azure (port 443) — works behind most firewalls. code-server: Linux server recommended (binary or Docker). ARM64 (aarch64) supported. No CUDA-specific compatibility issues — it is purely IDE/network layer. Known trap: VS Code Remote extensions must be installed separately on the remote host; not all marketplace extensions support remote execution (check 'extensionKind' in package.json).

items.name: TrueNAS SCALE (Fangtooth 25.04)
items.specs: Current release: TrueNAS 25.04 'Fangtooth' (April 2025); point updates through 2026. Linux kernel 6.12. ZFS with fast deduplication and 5x RAID-Z expansion acceleration vs. prior versions. Native LXC Linux system containers via Incus (replaces libvirt). NFS v4.1/v4.2 and SMB 3.1.1 shares with improved metadata performance (up to 10x faster directory listings vs. 13.x). Docker app catalog integrated (Electric Eel 24.10 added this; Fangtooth refines it). Previous Dragonfish 24.04: 50% higher IOPS on same hardware vs. TrueNAS 13.0. Per-app IP address selection. Web UI management.
items.price_band_usd: Free (BSL/open core). TrueNAS Enterprise hardware appliances: $3,000+ from iXsystems, but SCALE software itself is free to install on commodity hardware.
items.ai_relevance: Provides the shared model-weight and dataset storage layer for a multi-rig lab. Large LLM weight files (10–70 GB per model) stored once on TrueNAS NFS share, mounted into all Docker containers / Kubernetes pods / VMs across every developer's workload — no per-machine re-downloads. ZFS data integrity prevents silent corruption of model weights. Centralized Ollama model directories are a documented use case. For a 3–4 dev shared rig: TrueNAS runs on a separate storage server (or a Proxmox VM with ZFS pool passthrough), exports NFS to the GPU rig. Also serves dataset shares for training jobs and backup snapshots of user home directories.
items.compatibility: x86_64 (primary). ARM support experimental. Runs bare-metal or in a Proxmox VM with HBA/disk passthrough (recommended for ZFS). NVIDIA/AMD GPU passthrough to TrueNAS VMs possible but not the primary use case. NFS client compatibility: Linux (standard), Windows (NFS client built-in but SMB often preferred on Windows), macOS (NFS works natively). Known trap: Fangtooth 25.04 deprecated TrueCharts app catalog — migrate to official apps or alternative community catalogs. Electric Eel 24.10 to Fangtooth 25.04 upgrade path requires app migration.

items.name: Portainer
items.kind: container management UI / team access control plane
items.specs: Portainer CE (Community Edition): free, OSS (zlib license). Portainer BE (Business Edition): $149/node/year starting price for 5 nodes ($29/node/year equivalent). CE supports Docker, Docker Swarm, Kubernetes, Podman environments. BE adds RBAC with granular roles: Environment Admin, Operator (start/stop/redeploy, no create/delete), Helpdesk (read-only), Standard User (own resources), Read-Only User. Multi-tenancy via Teams + Environment-level access control pairings. Multi-host management from single UI. Stack deployment via Compose files. Log tailing, exec into containers, volume management across fleet. Current: Portainer 2.25.x (2026).
items.price_band_usd: CE: Free. BE: $149+ per node/year (5-node bundle; 3-node free trial available). Free tier includes 3 nodes in BE trial.
items.ai_relevance: The management UI and access governance layer for multi-developer Docker/Kubernetes environments. For a 3–4 dev shared rig: one developer is Environment Admin, others get Standard User or Operator roles — they can manage their own inference containers but cannot delete each other's deployments or modify system stacks. Teams map to project groups. Developers deploy vLLM/Ollama/ComfyUI stacks via the Portainer UI without SSH access to the host. Particularly useful when developers are not Linux-fluent. Portainer BE's Operator role is well-suited for devs who need to restart/redeploy inference services but should not touch infrastructure.
items.compatibility: Runs as a Docker container on any Linux/Windows/macOS Docker host. Manages remote Docker hosts via Portainer Agent (lightweight container on managed host). Kubernetes support: k3s, k8s, AKS, EKS. ARM64 (aarch64) images available. No CUDA/GPU-specific compatibility issues — Portainer is a management plane, not a runtime. Known trap: CE does not have RBAC — all authenticated users have equivalent access. Upgrade to BE or use Traefik + auth middleware for access control on CE.

items.name: 10GbE Networking (Switches + NICs)
items.specs: 10GbE switches: TP-Link TL-SX1008 8-port unmanaged 10GbE ~$319; TRENDnet TEG-S762 5x 10G ~$230; FS.com S3910-24TF managed 24-port 10GbE ~$399–$599; used Mikrotik CRS317-1G-16S+ (16x SFP+) ~$350–$450 used. NICs: Mellanox ConnectX-3 (used, SFP+, 10/40GbE) ~$15–$30 eBay; Intel X550-T2 dual 10GBASE-T ~$80–$120 retail; Mellanox ConnectX-4 Lx (25GbE SFP28, PCIe 3.0 x8) ~$50–$80 used. DAC cables (SFP+ passive): $10–$20 per link. 2.5GbE switches (SODOLA, TP-Link TL-SG108-M2) ~$60–$80 for 8-port — sufficient for NAS-to-rig links at lower cost.
items.price_band_usd: 10GbE switch: $230–$600 (new), $80–$450 (used/refurb). 10GbE NIC: $15–$120 depending on generation and port type. Full 4-node 10GbE fabric: ~$500–$800 total (used gear path).
items.ai_relevance: Network bandwidth is the hidden bottleneck in a shared-rig AI lab. Transferring a 40 GB model from TrueNAS NFS to GPU container over 1GbE takes ~5–6 minutes; over 10GbE it takes ~32 seconds. With 3–4 devs pulling different models from NAS simultaneously, 1GbE becomes a serious serialization bottleneck. 10GbE between GPU rig and NAS is the minimum recommended for a team workflow. The developer laptops only need 1GbE or Wi-Fi 6 (1.2 Gbps) for interactive IDE/terminal use — data stays on the server side. SFP+ DAC cables (NIC to switch direct) are the most cost-effective path: $20 NIC + $15 DAC = $35/node for 10Gbps.
items.compatibility: Intel X550: Linux/Windows/macOS, well-supported; ixgbe kernel driver on Linux (in-tree). Mellanox ConnectX-3/4: Linux excellent (mlx4/mlx5 drivers in-tree); Windows drivers available. SFP+ DAC: passive copper, works in any SFP+ port regardless of vendor. RDMA/RoCE: ConnectX-4+ supports RoCEv2 for NCCL/GPU-direct storage if needed. Known trap: 10GBASE-T (RJ45) NICs run hotter and draw more power (~10W) than SFP+ DAC links; avoid for dense rack builds. ARM (Raspberry Pi 5): only 1GbE on-board; USB 3.0 to 2.5GbE adapter is max practical.

items.name: Netdata + Prometheus/Grafana
items.specs: Netdata Agent: per-second metric collection; ~50 MB RAM. Native NVIDIA DCGM collector: hundreds of GPU metrics (utilization, memory, temperature, NVLink, MIG) per second. Intel GPU monitoring via igpu plugin. LLM inference monitoring: tracks GPU memory saturation, inference latency, throughput. Homelab plan: $90/year, unlimited nodes. Prometheus + Grafana stack: Prometheus scrapes metrics endpoints; Grafana visualizes with pre-built dashboards (NVIDIA DCGM Grafana dashboard #12239 widely used). Beszel: newer ultra-lightweight alternative (~10 MB agent) for basic CPU/RAM/disk metrics without GPU depth.
items.price_band_usd: Netdata OSS: free (no cloud features). Netdata Homelab plan: $90/yr. Prometheus + Grafana: both free OSS. Beszel: free OSS.
items.ai_relevance: Essential for a shared-rig lab: lets all developers see real-time GPU utilization, VRAM consumption, and inference throughput before launching a job that would OOM or thrash. Netdata DCGM collector catches thermal throttling and memory pressure before they cause inference failures. For a 4-dev shared rig: Grafana dashboard shows per-container GPU allocation so devs know when a GPU is free. Prometheus alerting fires a Slack/webhook when VRAM exceeds 90% — prevents runaway training jobs from killing other devs' inference. Netdata also monitors TrueNAS NFS bandwidth and Docker container resource usage in the same view.
items.compatibility: Linux primary (agent is native). Windows: Netdata via WSL or Docker container. NVIDIA DCGM: requires DCGM installed on host (Linux only, CUDA 11.0+ supported). AMD ROCm: rocm-smi-exporter for Prometheus exists but less polished than NVIDIA DCGM. ARM64 (aarch64): Netdata supports. Grafana: any OS (Docker recommended). Known trap: DCGM requires the DCGM host engine service running alongside the driver — adds ~200 MB RAM overhead on the host.

items.name: Headscale (Self-Hosted Tailscale Control Server)
items.specs: Open source re-implementation of the Tailscale coordination server, written in Go. Single binary; ~30 MB. Single tailnet scope. Features: node registration, IP allocation (100.x.x.x), MagicDNS, ACLs, pre-auth keys, OIDC/SSO, ephemeral nodes, Taildrop file sharing, embedded DERP relay server. Uses standard Tailscale clients on all developer devices — no custom client needed. Current: v0.23.x (active development as of 2026). Maintained by juanfont/headscale on GitHub (12k+ stars).
items.price_band_usd: Free (BSD 3-Clause OSS). Self-host on any VPS (~$5/month DigitalOcean/Hetzner) or on the homelab itself.
items.ai_relevance: Preferred over commercial Tailscale for teams that want zero dependency on external cloud control planes. All mesh VPN coordination stays on infrastructure the team owns. Identical developer experience to Tailscale (same clients on laptops/phones). Enables the same 'access GPU rig from any laptop' topology as Tailscale. Suitable for privacy-sensitive AI workloads (proprietary model weights, client data) where traffic metadata in a cloud control plane is unacceptable. For a 4-dev team: run Headscale on a Proxmox LXC container on the homelab; all dev laptops join as nodes; GPU rig is a tagged resource with ACL restricting access to team members only.
items.compatibility: Linux server (recommended). Docker image available. Tailscale client: Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android — all official Tailscale clients work with Headscale. Limitation vs. commercial Tailscale: no subnet router GUI (manual config), no Tailscale Funnel (public HTTPS), no MagicDNS split-horizon for internet names. DERP relay: Headscale includes embedded DERP; can also point to Tailscale's global DERP infrastructure for latency optimization.

items.name: code-server / Coder
items.kind: self-hosted browser-based IDE / developer workspace platform
items.specs: code-server: VS Code running in a browser, self-hosted (Go + Node). Single-user or multi-user via reverse proxy + auth. Coder platform: open source workspace orchestration layer on top of code-server; provisions developer workspaces via Terraform templates on Docker, Kubernetes, or bare metal. Workspace resources (CPU, RAM, GPU) defined per template. Current: code-server 4.x; Coder v2.x. JetBrains Gateway also integrates with Coder for IntelliJ/PyCharm remote IDEs. Workspaces are persistent containers — developer state survives laptop closure.
items.price_band_usd: code-server: free OSS (MIT). Coder OSS (self-hosted): free. Coder Cloud: custom enterprise pricing.
items.ai_relevance: Superior to VS Code Remote Tunnels for a managed team environment: Coder lets the lab admin define workspace templates with pre-installed CUDA toolkits, model libraries, and GPU quotas — developers spin up a workspace from a menu and get a browser IDE with GPU access in seconds. No laptop-side software required beyond a browser. Ideal for onboarding new developers or contractors who should not have SSH keys. GPU-enabled workspace templates specify --gpus device=<id> in the Coder Docker backend. Model sizes: same as the GPU allocated to the workspace (1x RTX 4090 = up to ~24 GB VRAM for inference or fine-tuning).
items.compatibility: Server: Linux x86_64 or aarch64 (Docker or bare metal). Client: any modern browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari). NVIDIA GPU: NVIDIA CT required on server; works via Docker backend. ROCm: ROCm Docker images work with Coder Docker backend. macOS server: supported but NVIDIA GPU unavailable. Known trap: code-server uses significant RAM for each active user session (~300–500 MB per IDE); plan RAM accordingly for 3–4 concurrent developers.

summary: A 3–4 developer shared-inference homelab in 2026 is best built in layers: Proxmox VE 9.1 as the bare-metal hypervisor (PCIe passthrough for GPU isolation per VM/LXC container); Docker + NVIDIA Container Toolkit as the per-workload runtime; TrueNAS SCALE Fangtooth 25.04 exporting NFS for centralized model-weight storage (preventing repeated 40 GB downloads per developer); Tailscale or self-hosted Headscale as the zero-config mesh VPN giving every developer secure laptop-to-rig access without firewall changes; and VS Code Remote SSH / code-server/Coder for IDE access to the remote GPU containers. JupyterHub (on Docker or k3s) is the cleanest single-tool solution for data-science teams that want browser-based GPU notebooks with per-user resource quotas. k3s replaces Docker Compose when teams need self-healing across two or more rigs. Portainer BE adds RBAC and a management UI for teams where not everyone should have SSH access. 10GbE networking (SFP+ DAC, ~$35/node on used gear) between NAS and GPU rig is the single highest-ROI infrastructure upgrade — it collapses model-load time from minutes to seconds. Netdata + DCGM provides the per-second GPU visibility needed to coordinate shared hardware without collisions. All core software tools (Proxmox, k3s, JupyterHub, Headscale, code-server, Portainer CE, TrueNAS SCALE, Netdata OSS) are free and open source; the only recurring costs are optional Tailscale team plans ($8–18/user/month) or Portainer BE ($149/node/yr) for teams that want managed RBAC without self-hosting the control planes.

items.name: Market Context: June 2026 Memory Shortage — Buy vs Wait
items.specs: GDDR7/GDDR6X VRAM now >80% of GPU bill-of-materials. NVIDIA confirmed 30-40% output cuts on mid-range RTX 50-series (5070/5060 Ti) to prioritize Blackwell AI accelerators and higher-margin SKUs. Blackwell PRO GPU lead times are 3-7 months. RTX 5090 street price ~$3,000-$3,700 (150-185% above $1,999 MSRP). RTX 5080 running ~45% above $999 MSRP. Market tightness is structural and forecast to persist into at least H1 2027.
items.ai_relevance: Every AI/LLM build is affected. If you have immediate productive workload (model inference, local dev, fine-tuning), buy now — lost compute time costs more than the premium. If no active workload until Q3 2026+, wait-and-watch is defensible but risky given no clear supply relief date. The AMD RDNA4 stack (RX 9070 XT) is the one segment with relative inventory normalcy.
items.compatibility: Windows/Linux both viable. CUDA path remains dominant for training; ROCm 7.2 (March 2026) finally achieved Ollama/llama.cpp/vLLM parity on RDNA3/RDNA4 — the AMD penalty is mostly gone for inference. Vulkan fallback works cross-platform for llama.cpp on AMD when ROCm is unavailable (Windows-friendly).
items.price_band_usd: N/A — market intelligence tier

items.name: Tier 1 — $1,000-$2,000 Starter: AMD RX 9070 XT Single-GPU Node
items.specs: RX 9070 XT: 16GB GDDR6, 256-bit bus, RDNA4 architecture, 1,557 TOPS INT4 with sparsity, ~$649 street (MSRP $599, modest markup vs NVIDIA). Pair with AMD Ryzen 7 or Intel Core i7, 32GB DDR5, 1TB NVMe. Total system build ~$1,400-$1,800. For pure GPU add-in to existing rig: $649-$700.
items.ai_relevance: Handles 7B models at full FP16 and 13B-14B at Q4 quantization comfortably in 16GB. ~80-100 tokens/sec on 7B (Llama 3/Mistral). Tight on 30B+ models — they spill to system RAM and performance craters. Best fit: solo developer doing daily LLM-assisted coding, RAG pipelines, smaller fine-tunes. Not suitable for 70B inference at usable speeds.
items.compatibility: ROCm 7.2+ on Linux: full first-class Ollama/llama.cpp/vLLM support without HSA_OVERRIDE workarounds as of March 2026. Windows: llama.cpp Vulkan backend works well; ROCm for Windows is still experimental. No CUDA — some Python ML wheels (e.g., Flash Attention 2 CUDA kernels) require workarounds. Avoid if PyTorch CUDA training is the primary workload.
items.price_band_usd: $649-$1,800 (GPU alone vs full build)

items.name: Tier 1B — $1,500-$2,000 Starter: Mac Studio M4 Max 36-64GB
items.specs: M4 Max chip: 14-core CPU, 32-core GPU, 16-core Neural Engine. 36GB unified memory base config ($1,999); upgradeable to 64GB ($2,399) or 128GB ($3,199 for 16-core CPU M4 Max variant). Memory bandwidth: ~546 GB/s (M4 Max). Fanless-quiet, 90W idle, macOS-only.
items.ai_relevance: Unified memory means the Neural Engine and GPU share the same pool — a 36GB M4 Max runs 34B models in Q4 at ~30-35 tokens/sec; 70B Q4 fits in 64GB config. Apple's MLX framework and llama.cpp Metal backend are mature. For developers primarily on macOS, this is the cleanest sub-$2K path to 70B capability. Note: M5 Max/Ultra reportedly planned for late 2026; supply constraints may push to Q4.
items.compatibility: macOS only (Metal/MLX). PyTorch MPS backend works for fine-tuning but is slower than CUDA for most training tasks. No ROCm/CUDA. llama.cpp Metal backend: excellent. Ollama on Mac: excellent. Docker runs via Linux VM (Rosetta). Best for inference-only or RAG workloads; suboptimal if CUDA-specific training code is a requirement.

items.name: Tier 2 — $3,000-$5,000 Mid: RTX 5090 Single-GPU Workstation
items.specs: RTX 5090: 32GB GDDR7, 1,792 GB/s bandwidth, Blackwell GB202, 575W TDP, PCIe 5.0. Street price ~$3,000-$3,700 (MSRP $1,999 Founders Edition, which clears in minutes). Full workstation (Ryzen 9 7950X or Intel Core i9, 64GB DDR5, 2TB NVMe, 1000W PSU): $4,500-$5,500 total. GPU alone at MSRP if you can catch a Founders Edition restock: $1,999.
items.ai_relevance: Best consumer card for LLM inference in 2026 per bandwidth/VRAM. Runs 32B models at Q4 ~45 tokens/sec; 70B Q4 fits only with aggressive Q3/Q4 quantization (marginally, ~35-38GB — watch model-specific overhead). 70B at Q4 can spill if context is large; for reliable 70B you want dual 5090 or jump to professional tier. Excellent for 7B-34B range, Stable Diffusion, code gen, RAG.
items.compatibility: CUDA 12.8+, compute capability sm_120 (Blackwell). Windows 11 / Ubuntu 22.04+ supported. Verify PyTorch nightly builds support sm_120 before purchasing if fine-tuning is the use case — stable PyTorch releases may lag new architectures by 2-4 months. No NVLink on consumer cards — multi-GPU requires PCIe tensor parallelism (vLLM/llama.cpp supported, ~85-92% efficiency).

items.name: Tier 2B — $3,000-$5,000 Mid: Used RTX 4090 + RTX 6000 Ada Value Play
items.specs: RTX 4090: 24GB GDDR6X, 1,008 GB/s, $1,100-$1,500 used (street is elevated from GDDR6X shortage repricing). RTX 6000 Ada: 48GB GDDR6 ECC, 960 GB/s, Ada Lovelace (sm_89), 300W TDP — used/refurb ~$5,000-$6,000; new retail ~$7,089. RTX 6000 Ada is the sub-$10K single card that handles 70B Q4 reliably in 48GB without quantization compromise.
items.ai_relevance: RTX 4090 at $1,100-$1,500 used is the workhorse for <30B models; pairs well with CPU offload for 70B. RTX 6000 Ada at 48GB is the sweet spot if 70B reliable inference is the target and a Blackwell PRO 6000 ($8-9K+) is out of budget. Workstation drivers + ISV certs make Ada 6000 the preferred card in professional/enterprise environments. ECC memory adds reliability for long-running inference servers.
items.compatibility: Both Ada Lovelace (sm_89): full CUDA 12.x support, stable PyTorch wheels, Flash Attention 2 supported. RTX 4090 consumer card: GeForce drivers, no ECC. RTX 6000 Ada: workstation drivers, ECC, certified for VMware/Citrix vGPU (paid license). Both cards fully supported in Ollama, vLLM, llama.cpp. RTX 4090 SLI/NVLink: not supported. RTX 6000 Ada NVLink: not supported in workstation edition.

items.name: Tier 3 — $10,000-$12,000 Shared Team: Dual RTX 5090 or RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Node
items.specs: Option A — Dual RTX 5090: 64GB GDDR7 combined, 3,584 GB/s combined bandwidth (PCIe connected, no NVLink). Full workstation (HEDT platform, Threadripper or Xeon W, 128GB DDR5, 2x 2TB NVMe RAID, 1600W PSU): ~$9,000-$12,000. Option B — Single RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell: 96GB GDDR7 ECC, 24,064 CUDA cores, 600W TDP, GB202 die. MSRP ~$8,435-$8,565 at launch; NVIDIA Marketplace listing June 2026 at $13,250; retail ~$8,000-$9,200. Full system ~$10,000-$12,000.
items.ai_relevance: For a 3-8 person dev team running shared vLLM or Ollama inference: dual 5090 serves 70B Q4 at reasonable multi-user batch throughput; RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell is the cleaner option (96GB unified, ECC, NVLink-capable in server config) for 70B FP16 or 100B+ Q4 MoE models. Both architectures support continuous batching via vLLM. Developer GPU utilization is bursty (5-15% average), so a single well-specced node handles 5-15 concurrent light users.
items.compatibility: Dual 5090: no NVLink — software tensor parallelism only (vLLM tensor_parallel_size=2, llama.cpp with --tensor-split). RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell: sm_120 (Blackwell), CUDA 12.8+, workstation drivers, ECC, VMware vGPU ready. Both Windows Server 2022 / Ubuntu 22.04 LTS supported. Prebuilt system integrators: BIZON, Puget Systems, VRLA Tech (Lambda Labs exited on-prem hardware as of August 2025). Team needs Docker + vLLM or LiteLLM proxy for multi-user access control.
items.price_band_usd: $9,000-$13,000 (dual 5090 system); $10,000-$13,000 (single PRO 6000 Blackwell system)

items.name: Tier 4 — $20,000+ Shared Team / Production: Used H100 SXM5 or New PRO 6000 Multi-GPU
items.specs: Used H100 SXM5 80GB: ~$6,000-$15,000 per card (dramatic drop from $40K peak in 2023 as Blackwell displaces). H100 PCIe 80GB used: $15,000-$20,000 per card. A100 80GB used: $4,000-$9,000. A 2x used H100 SXM5 bare-metal node runs ~$20,000-$30,000 plus server chassis (Dell PowerEdge XE9680 or SuperMicro SYS-420GP). New 4x RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell (384GB GDDR7 total): ~$34,000-$40,000 in parts.
items.ai_relevance: Used H100 SXM5 is the most bandwidth-dense option at this tier (4,800 GB/s HBM3e vs 1,792 GB/s GDDR7 on RTX 5090). For 70B FP8 inference: H100 delivers ~120 tokens/sec vs ~32 tokens/sec on PRO 6000 Blackwell. Critical for: multi-user 70B+ production serving, fine-tuning runs >7B, MoE models (Mixtral/Llama 4 Scout). Expect another 10-20% price drop on H100 secondary market through 2026 as Blackwell GA widens.
items.compatibility: H100 SXM5: requires NVSwitch/NVLink fabric (HGX chassis) or NVLink bridge for multi-GPU unified memory — cannot use in standard PCIe workstation. H100 PCIe: standard server slot but no NVLink pooling. CUDA 12.x, sm_90a. A100 (sm_80): still fully supported, mature toolchain, excellent for inference but lower bandwidth. Secondary market sourcing: Compute Exchange, BigDataSupply, HashRateIndex, Introl. Request GPU health logs (GPU burn test results, hours-of-use) before purchase — no warranty on most gray-market H100s.

items.name: New vs Used Tradeoff Framework
items.specs: New RTX 5090 (32GB GDDR7, Blackwell sm_120): $3,000-$3,700 street — gets you latest CUDA arch, full PyTorch support, warranty, no history unknowns. Used RTX 4090 (24GB, sm_89): $1,100-$1,500 — proven Ada architecture, mature toolchain, ~30-40% cheaper than 5090, loses ~15% bandwidth but still capable. Used A100 80GB (sm_80): $4,000-$9,000 — no consumer driver overhead, ECC, NVLink in server configs, but 2020-era architecture; still faster than RTX 5090 for 70B inference due to HBM bandwidth (2,000 GB/s). Secondary market H100: see pricing above — hold value well (60-80% of original) but declining as Blackwell ships.
items.ai_relevance: Rule of thumb: if the workload is pure inference (Ollama/vLLM serving), older but higher-bandwidth server GPUs (A100, H100) beat newer consumer cards at comparable or lower cost. For development + training + inference mixed use, new consumer or pro cards win on driver stability and CUDA ecosystem. Used enterprise GPUs require Linux + familiarity with server drivers; they are not plug-and-play on Windows desktops.
items.compatibility: Used server GPUs (A100, H100) are passively cooled blower cards for data center chassis — require active cooling solutions or chassis support fans for homelab (PCIe riser + custom bracket + 80mm fan array is common). No display output. Driver stack: CUDA, nvidia-smi, standard. NOT suitable for Windows gaming or display use. Refurb vendors: Compute Exchange, BigDataSupply, Introl, ServerMonkey (for Dell PowerEdge chassis). Check GPU burn results and VBIOS version before buying.
items.price_band_usd: Varies — see per-tier entries; used multiplier is ~0.5-0.75x of original MSRP depending on generation and condition

items.name: When to Lock Memory Purchases — Procurement Timing
items.specs: GDDR7 and GDDR6X are supply-constrained because HBM demand (for datacenter) competes for wafer capacity at SK Hynix/Samsung/Micron. The shortage was declared by multiple vendors in Q4 2025 and remains active as of June 2026. Street prices across the RTX 50-series are 45-185% above MSRP. Analysts consensus: no meaningful supply relief until H2 2027 at earliest. GDDR7 allocation priority goes to AI accelerators (Blackwell GB100/GB200) over consumer cards.
items.ai_relevance: Lock in purchases now if: (1) you have an active project with weekly productive use, (2) you're treating it as depreciable business equipment (see tax tier), (3) your needed SKU is currently in stock anywhere. Defer if: (1) your use case starts Q3 2026+, (2) you can use cloud GPU rental in the interim (RTX 6000 Ada cloud ~$0.51-$0.90/hr, H100 cloud widely available), (3) you specifically need the RTX 5070 mid-range which may normalize by late 2026. Watch Founders Edition restock alerts (GPU Sniper, TrackaLacker) for MSRP purchases.
items.compatibility: MicroCenter in-store pickup is often the fastest MSRP path for RTX 5090/5080 restocks (US only, requires physical store visit). Newegg AIB models run $3,700+ currently. B&H and Adorama carry pro-tier cards (RTX 6000 Ada, PRO 6000 Blackwell) with more reliable stock than consumer cards. For used enterprise: Compute Exchange and BigDataSupply have volume inventory with negotiable pricing for multi-card orders.

items.name: Section 179 + Bonus Depreciation for US LLCs — 2026 Tax Note
items.specs: Section 179 limit for tax year 2026: $2,560,000 (dollar-for-dollar phase-out above $4,090,000 of property placed in service). Bonus Depreciation: restored to 100% for property acquired and placed in service after January 19, 2025, under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA). MACRS recovery period for computers/peripherals: 5-year property (GDS). Half-year convention applies in most cases. Computer hardware, GPU workstations, and AI accelerators qualify as 5-year MACRS property eligible for Sec. 179 and/or bonus depreciation.
items.ai_relevance: A US LLC buying a $10,000 GPU workstation can deduct the full $10,000 in year 1 via either Sec. 179 (subject to business income limitation — cannot create a loss) or 100% bonus depreciation (not limited by business income, can create/pass through a loss). For a pass-through LLC, this flows to the member's personal return. Critical: both the acquisition date (binding contract) and placed-in-service date must be on or after January 19, 2025. Timing hardware purchases before December 31, 2026 locks in the deduction for tax year 2026.
items.compatibility: Sec. 179 income limitation: the deduction cannot exceed net income from active business conduct — excess carries forward. Bonus depreciation: no income cap, but the property must be used >50% for business. If the rig doubles as a personal gaming machine, business-use percentage matters. Mixed-use allocation applies. Consult CPA for state conformity (some states do not conform to bonus depreciation). New or used hardware both qualify for bonus depreciation (post-2025 acquisition rule confirmed under OBBBA).
items.price_band_usd: N/A — tax strategy; effective after-tax cost of a $10K system at 37% top bracket = ~$6,300

items.name: Vendor Sourcing Map — Where to Actually Buy
items.specs: New consumer GPUs: MicroCenter (in-store MSRP restocks, best option for RTX 5090/5080 at list price), Newegg (AIB markup $3,700+ for 5090), B&H Photo (reliable stock for pro cards). New pro/workstation GPUs: B&H, Adorama, PNY direct (RTX 6000 Ada, PRO 6000 Blackwell). Prebuilt AI workstations: BIZON (volume GPU purchasing advantage), Puget Systems (premium support, tested configs), VRLA Tech (custom multi-GPU builds). Used enterprise GPUs: Compute Exchange (largest US secondary AI GPU marketplace), BigDataSupply, HashRateIndex/Braiins, Introl. Used consumer GPUs: eBay (price-check but no warranty), refurb Dell servers: ServerMonkey, PCSP, ExpressComputerSystems.
items.ai_relevance: Lambda Labs exited on-prem hardware sales as of August 2025 — no longer a source for workstations or servers. BIZON and Puget Systems both buy GPU inventory in volume and sometimes offer system pricing at or below self-build cost during shortage periods. For used H100/A100: Compute Exchange provides GPU health documentation (burn test, hours-of-use) — essential for enterprise-grade used hardware. For RTX 5090 MSRP alerts: GPU Sniper and TrackaLacker are the primary tools.
items.compatibility: MicroCenter: US stores only, in-person pickup often required for restock items. Newegg: ships nationally but premium AIB markup is the norm. Compute Exchange: typically ships via insured freight for high-value GPU cards. BIZON/Puget: 2-4 week build times; verify they stock Blackwell cards currently given supply constraints. Gray-market international re-exports of H100/A100: subject to US BIS export controls — buy only from US domestic vendors to avoid compliance risk.

summary: June 2026 homelab/AI-rig procurement is defined by a structural GDDR7/GDDR6X memory shortage that has pushed RTX 5090 street prices 150-185% above MSRP and created 3-7 month lead times on Blackwell PRO cards. The shortage is forecast to persist through at least H1 2027. Key strategic conclusions: (1) Buy now if you have active productive use — cloud GPU rental burn exceeds hardware premium for regular users. (2) AMD RX 9070 XT at ~$649 is the only consumer tier with near-normal pricing and is now fully supported under ROCm 7.2 for Ollama/llama.cpp/vLLM inference. (3) For $3-5K mid-tier, the RTX 5090 is the best single-card option but requires catching Founders Edition restocks at MSRP via GPU Sniper/TrackaLacker or accepting $3,000-$3,700 AIB pricing; RTX 6000 Ada used (~$5K) is the 48GB alternative. (4) For $10-12K shared-team use, RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell (96GB, ~$8-9K card) is cleaner than dual RTX 5090 because of unified VRAM and ECC. (5) Used H100 SXM5 at $6-15K each is the bandwidth king for 70B+ production inference, having dropped dramatically from $40K peak. (6) For US LLCs: 100% bonus depreciation is restored under the OBBBA for hardware placed in service after Jan 19, 2025 — a $10K GPU system at 37% bracket has an effective after-tax cost of ~$6,300; no income cap applies (unlike Sec. 179). (7) Lambda Labs exited on-prem hardware in August 2025; BIZON and Puget Systems are the primary prebuilt integrators. Source hardware from Compute Exchange or BigDataSupply for used enterprise cards with documented health logs.
